los.statement: Describe the structure and function of the GI system from a clinical perspective
Identify the principles of GI pathophysiology
Identify the clinical disease manifestations of the GI system

items.lo: Describe the structure and function of the GI system from a clinical perspective
items.text: Submucosal (Meissner) plexus: sits in the submucosa and controls secretion and local blood flow. It is the plexus that talks to glands.

items.lo: Describe the structure and function of the GI system from a clinical perspective
items.text: Myenteric (Auerbach) plexus: sits between the inner circular and outer longitudinal muscle of the muscularis externa and controls motility. It is the plexus that talks to muscle.

items.lo: Describe the structure and function of the GI system from a clinical perspective
items.text: Why this is testable: patients with complete spinal cord transection above the sacral outflow still digest food. The ENS keeps going. The CNS just changes how vigorously.

items.lo: Describe the structure and function of the GI system from a clinical perspective
items.text: PNS (vagus + pelvic nerves): increases motility, secretion, and sphincter relaxation. Vagus covers everything from esophagus through the proximal two-thirds of the colon. Pelvic nerves (S2–S4) cover the distal one-third of colon, rectum, and internal anal sphincter.

items.lo: Describe the structure and function of the GI system from a clinical perspective
items.text: SNS (T5–L2 via splanchnics): decreases motility and secretion, contracts sphincters, and vasoconstricts splanchnic vessels — that last point is why GI ischemia is a feature of shock states.

items.lo: Identify the principles of GI pathophysiology
items.text: Normal → metaplasia: one mature cell type is replaced by another mature cell type better suited to the stress. Reversible if the stressor is removed.

items.lo: Identify the principles of GI pathophysiology
items.text: Metaplasia → dysplasia: the replacement tissue starts showing disordered growth and nuclear atypia. Still potentially reversible early; high-grade dysplasia is treated as pre-malignant.

items.lo: Identify the principles of GI pathophysiology
items.text: Dysplasia → carcinoma: frank invasion through the basement membrane. Irreversible.

items.lo: Identify the principles of GI pathophysiology
items.text: Upper esophageal sphincter (UES): skeletal muscle, voluntary initiation of swallow. Failure here = oropharyngeal dysphagia.

items.lo: Identify the principles of GI pathophysiology
items.text: Lower esophageal sphincter (LES): smooth muscle, inhibitory innervation via NO/VIP from myenteric (Auerbach) neurons. Lose those inhibitory neurons → LES never relaxes → achalasia.

items.lo: Identify the principles of GI pathophysiology
items.text: Motility dysphagia: trouble with both solids and liquids from the start. The mechanism is failure of coordinated peristalsis or sphincter relaxation. Classic example: achalasia.

items.lo: Identify the principles of GI pathophysiology
items.text: Obstructive (mechanical) dysphagia: trouble with solids first, then progressing to liquids. Something is narrowing the lumen and the narrowing gets worse. Classic examples: esophageal cancer, peptic stricture, Schatzki ring, esophageal web.

items.lo: Identify the clinical disease manifestations of the GI system
items.text: Vitamin B12 deficiency: smooth, beefy-red tongue + megaloblastic anemia + neurologic symptoms (subacute combined degeneration). The neuro piece is what separates B12 from folate.

items.lo: Identify the clinical disease manifestations of the GI system
items.text: Iron deficiency anemia: atrophic glossitis + angular cheilitis + koilonychia. When you add an upper esophageal web you get Plummer-Vinson syndrome (see below).

items.lo: Identify the clinical disease manifestations of the GI system
items.text: Scarlet fever: strawberry tongue in a child with pharyngitis and a sandpaper rash. Group A strep.

items.lo: Identify the clinical disease manifestations of the GI system
items.text: Niacin (B3) deficiency: pellagra — the 4 D's (dermatitis, diarrhea, dementia, death) with glossitis as a feature.

items.lo: Identify the clinical disease manifestations of the GI system
items.text: Presentation: dysphagia to both solids and liquids from onset, regurgitation of undigested food, weight loss.

items.lo: Identify the clinical disease manifestations of the GI system
items.text: Imaging (barium swallow shows the classic "bird's beak" — dilated esophagus tapering to a narrow LES.)

items.lo: Identify the clinical disease manifestations of the GI system
items.text: Confirm with: esophageal manometry — incomplete LES relaxation and aperistalsis. Manometry is the gold standard.

items.lo: Identify the clinical disease manifestations of the GI system
items.text: GERD: heartburn, regurgitation, worse when supine or after meals. Empiric PPI is reasonable initial management.

items.lo: Identify the clinical disease manifestations of the GI system
items.text: Alarm features: dysphagia, odynophagia, weight loss, anemia, hematemesis, age >50 with new symptoms — these mandate endoscopy.

items.lo: Identify the clinical disease manifestations of the GI system
items.text: Barrett's esophagus: intestinal metaplasia of the distal esophagus. Confirmed on biopsy by goblet cells. Surveillance endoscopy at defined intervals.

items.lo: Identify the clinical disease manifestations of the GI system
items.text: Esophageal adenocarcinoma: the feared endpoint. Distal third of esophagus. Risk factors: chronic GERD, obesity, smoking, male sex.

items.lo: Identify the clinical disease manifestations of the GI system
items.text: Sliding (Type I, ~95%): the GE junction slides up into the chest through the hiatus. Presents with reflux symptoms. Managed medically — PPIs, lifestyle.

items.lo: Identify the clinical disease manifestations of the GI system
items.text: Paraesophageal (Type II–IV, ~5%): the GE junction stays put; the fundus herniates alongside the esophagus through the hiatus. Risk of strangulation / volvulus / ischemia. Surgical repair even when asymptomatic in selected cases.

items.lo: Identify the clinical disease manifestations of the GI system
items.text: Peptic ulcer disease (PUD): epigastric pain. Duodenal ulcer: pain relieved by food (food triggers acid neutralization). Gastric ulcer: pain worsened by food. Both have H. pylori as the dominant cause; NSAIDs second.

items.lo: Identify the clinical disease manifestations of the GI system
items.text: Crohn's vs UC (one-liner): Crohn's: mouth-to-anus, skip lesions, transmural, fistulas, RLQ pain. UC: rectum upward, continuous, mucosal-only, bloody diarrhea, increased colon cancer risk. Full coverage in Lecs 19–20.

items.lo: Identify the clinical disease manifestations of the GI system
items.text: Hemorrhoids: Internal (above the dentate line): painless bleeding, visceral innervation. External (below the dentate line): painful, somatic innervation. Management differs accordingly.

los.statement: Describe what GI hormones are and which cells produce/release them
Describe what regulates the secretion of digestive hormones
Describe the functions of digestive hormones (gastrin family)
Describe the mechanism of HCl secretion (parietal cell pump)
Describe the mechanism of action of acid-suppressing drugs

items.topic: What makes something a 'GI hormone' for exam purposes
items.lo: Describe what GI hormones are and which cells produce/release them
items.text: Hormone vs paracrine — the testable line: hormones use the BLOODSTREAM to reach distant targets; paracrines diffuse locally through interstitial fluid. Histamine in the stomach is the canonical paracrine.

items.lo: Describe what GI hormones are and which cells produce/release them
items.text: Secretin → duodenal S cells (triggered by acid pH <4.5)

items.lo: Describe what GI hormones are and which cells produce/release them
items.text: CCK → duodenal I cells (triggered by fats and proteins)

items.lo: Describe what GI hormones are and which cells produce/release them
items.text: GIP → duodenal K cells (triggered by glucose; the 'incretin')

items.lo: Describe what GI hormones are and which cells produce/release them
items.text: Motilin → duodenal M cells (between-meal MMC waves)

items.lo: Describe what GI hormones are and which cells produce/release them
items.text: Gastrin → gastric G cells of the antrum (triggered by peptides + distention + vagal GRP)

items.lo: Describe what GI hormones are and which cells produce/release them
items.text: Somatostatin → gastric D cells (the universal GI inhibitor)

items.lo: Describe what GI hormones are and which cells produce/release them
items.text: Histamine → gastric ECL cells (paracrine, not hormone, but in the same family on exam questions)

items.topic: Secretin — the bicarbonate response to acid
items.lo: Describe what regulates the secretion of digestive hormones
items.text: Secretin trigger: duodenal pH <4.5. Target: pancreatic duct cells. Effect: bicarbonate-rich secretion to neutralize acid.

items.topic: Secretin — the bicarbonate response to acid
items.lo: Describe what regulates the secretion of digestive hormones
items.text: Duct cells secrete bicarbonate, NOT enzymes. Enzyme secretion is the acinar-cell job, stimulated by CCK. This duct-vs-acinar split is one of Potter's most-flagged distinctions.

items.lo: Describe what regulates the secretion of digestive hormones
items.text: VIPoma → WDHA syndrome (Watery Diarrhea, Hypokalemia, Achlorhydria), also called Verner-Morrison syndrome or pancreatic cholera. Pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor.

items.lo: Describe what regulates the secretion of digestive hormones
items.text: Confirm a VIPoma with a serum VIP level. Chromogranin A is a non-specific neuroendocrine marker.

items.lo: Describe what regulates the secretion of digestive hormones
items.text: GIP source: K cells. Trigger: glucose. Target: pancreatic beta cells → insulin release. Mnemonic: GIP = Glucose-Insulinotropic Peptide.

items.lo: Describe what regulates the secretion of digestive hormones
items.text: Guanylin receptor = guanylyl cyclase C. Effect = cGMP rise → Cl/water secretion. Same target as E. coli ST toxin (traveler's diarrhea) and linaclotide (IBS-C drug).

items.topic: Gastrin — the master regulator of gastric acid
items.lo: Describe the functions of digestive hormones (gastrin family)
items.text: Three gastrin triggers: peptides/amino acids + gastric distention + vagal GRP. Acid in the antrum INHIBITS gastrin via somatostatin.

items.topic: Gastrin — the master regulator of gastric acid
items.lo: Describe the functions of digestive hormones (gastrin family)
items.text: Gastrin acts via CCK-B (= CCK2) receptor on parietal cells. Same receptor family as CCK but the B subtype is the gastrin-preferring one.

items.lo: Describe the functions of digestive hormones (gastrin family)
items.text: ZE diagnostic clues: recurrent ulcers in atypical locations + PPI failure + serum gastrin >1000 pg/mL (often much higher). Confirm with secretin stimulation test (paradoxical gastrin rise in ZE).

items.lo: Describe the functions of digestive hormones (gastrin family)
items.text: ZE + MEN1 association: ~25% of gastrinomas occur in MEN1 syndrome (parathyroid + pancreas + pituitary tumors). Screen with PTH, calcium, prolactin.

items.lo: Describe the functions of digestive hormones (gastrin family)
items.text: CCK = Contract gallbladder, Open sphincter of Oddi, K-ick the pancreas (enzyme release). Memorize this mnemonic; it covers the 3 testable actions.

items.lo: Describe the functions of digestive hormones (gastrin family)
items.text: CCK trigger: fats and proteins in the duodenum. Carbohydrates trigger GIP, NOT CCK. Acid triggers secretin.

items.lo: Describe the functions of digestive hormones (gastrin family)
items.text: CCK receptor split: CCK-A (CCK1) on gallbladder + pancreatic acinar cells + brainstem satiety. CCK-B (CCK2) is the gastrin receptor on parietal cells.

items.topic: CCK in the HIDA scan (clinical application)
items.lo: Describe the functions of digestive hormones (gastrin family)
items.text: HIDA + CCK in a normal patient: GB contracts, EF >35%. EF <35% suggests biliary dyskinesia.

items.lo: Describe the mechanism of HCl secretion (parietal cell pump)
items.text: Histamine → H2 receptor (Gs-coupled GPCR → adenylyl cyclase → ↑cAMP → PKA). Source: ECL cells (paracrine). Histamine is the amplifier — block it and the other two stimuli weaken.

items.lo: Describe the mechanism of HCl secretion (parietal cell pump)
items.text: Gastrin → CCK-B (CCK2) receptor (Gq-coupled GPCR → PLC → IP3/DAG → ↑Ca²�). Source: G cells (hormone, endocrine).

items.lo: Describe the mechanism of HCl secretion (parietal cell pump)
items.text: ACh → M3 muscarinic receptor (Gq-coupled GPCR → PLC → IP3/DAG → ↑Ca²�). Source: vagus nerve (neurocrine).

items.topic: The H+/K+ ATPase — the final common pathway
items.lo: Describe the mechanism of HCl secretion (parietal cell pump)
items.text: H+/K+ ATPase pumps H+ out, K+ in. Final common pathway for HCl secretion — regardless of which of the three receptors triggered the cell.

items.topic: The H+/K+ ATPase — the final common pathway
items.lo: Describe the mechanism of HCl secretion (parietal cell pump)
items.text: PPI mechanism: -prazoles are prodrugs activated by gastric acid → bind irreversibly via disulfide to a cysteine in the pump → enzyme must be replaced for acid secretion to resume (12-24h half-life of effect).

items.topic: The H+/K+ ATPase — the final common pathway
items.lo: Describe the mechanism of HCl secretion (parietal cell pump)
items.text: Carbonic anhydrase = the H+ source. CA inhibitors (acetazolamide) modestly reduce gastric acid as a side effect — not first-line therapy, but biologically real.

items.topic: The H+/K+ ATPase — the final common pathway
items.lo: Describe the mechanism of HCl secretion (parietal cell pump)
items.text: Alkaline tide: postprandial rise in venous bicarbonate from basolateral HCO₃� export. Mild, transient, asymptomatic — but a high-yield physiology concept.

items.topic: Intrinsic factor — the other parietal cell product
items.lo: Describe the mechanism of HCl secretion (parietal cell pump)
items.text: Pernicious anemia triad: B12 deficiency (megaloblastic, neuro symptoms) + achlorhydria + secondary hypergastrinemia. All three follow from parietal cell destruction.

items.lo: Describe the mechanism of HCl secretion (parietal cell pump)
items.text: NSAID ulcers mechanism: COX inhibition → loss of PGE2 → loss of mucosal protection + loss of acid suppression. PPIs counter the second arm; misoprostol (a PGE1 analog) counters both.

items.lo: Describe the mechanism of action of acid-suppressing drugs
items.text: Antacid mechanism = direct luminal chemical neutralization. No receptor, no pump targeting. Tums = CaCO₃; Maalox = Al(OH)₃ + Mg(OH)₂.

items.lo: Describe the mechanism of action of acid-suppressing drugs
items.text: Antacid side effects: aluminum → constipation; magnesium → diarrhea (combined to balance); calcium → milk-alkali syndrome at high doses; sodium bicarb → metabolic alkalosis + Na load.

items.topic: H2 receptor blockers — competitive H2 antagonism
items.lo: Describe the mechanism of action of acid-suppressing drugs
items.text: H2 blocker effect = remove the cAMP amplifier → weaken all three receptor pathways indirectly. Less powerful than PPI but faster onset.

items.topic: H2 receptor blockers — competitive H2 antagonism
items.lo: Describe the mechanism of action of acid-suppressing drugs
items.text: Cimetidine side effects (the reason it's been replaced): anti-androgen → gynecomastia, galactorrhea, impotence. Plus potent CYP450 inhibitor — many drug interactions (warfarin, phenytoin, theophylline). Famotidine is cleaner; use that.

items.topic: Proton pump inhibitors — the strongest acid suppression
items.lo: Describe the mechanism of action of acid-suppressing drugs
items.text: PPI mechanism: irreversible covalent inhibition of the H+/K+ ATPase. Final common pathway = maximum suppression.

items.topic: Proton pump inhibitors — the strongest acid suppression
items.lo: Describe the mechanism of action of acid-suppressing drugs
items.text: PPI clinical use: chronic GERD, erosive esophagitis, PUD, ZE (high doses), prevention of NSAID ulcers, stress ulcer prophylaxis in ICU patients.

items.lo: Describe the mechanism of action of acid-suppressing drugs
items.text: PPI long-term risks (mnemonic: 'BAD MICE'): B12 deficiency, Bone (osteoporosis), Acquired (CA-pneumonia), Difficile, Magnesium low, Interstitial nephritis (CKD), C-rebound. Mix as needed for memory.

los.statement: Microsporidiosis: GI fungal infection in immunocompromised patients
Fungal intoxications affecting the GI tract

items.topic: Microsporidia — the reclassification you must know
items.lo: Microsporidiosis: GI fungal infection in immunocompromised patients
items.text: Microsporidia = FUNGI (reclassified from protozoa). Obligate intracellular, spore-forming, ~1-2 µm spores. Costin made this the first teaching point of the lecture.

items.lo: Microsporidiosis: GI fungal infection in immunocompromised patients
items.text: Risk factor: immunocompromise (especially HIV with CD4 <100, organ transplant). Immunocompetent infections exist but are rare and self-limited.

items.lo: Microsporidiosis: GI fungal infection in immunocompromised patients
items.text: Transmission: fecal-oral via contaminated water/food. Spores survive in the environment for months.

items.lo: Microsporidiosis: GI fungal infection in immunocompromised patients
items.text: Mechanism: spore polar-filament eversion → direct cytoplasmic injection of sporoplasm → intracellular replication → enterocyte rupture → villous atrophy → diarrhea.

items.lo: Microsporidiosis: GI fungal infection in immunocompromised patients
items.text: Classic presentation: HIV patient with low CD4 + chronic non-bloody watery diarrhea + weight loss + fat malabsorption.

items.lo: Microsporidiosis: GI fungal infection in immunocompromised patients
items.text: Diagnostic stain: modified trichrome (Weber) — pink-red spores on green background.

items.lo: Microsporidiosis: GI fungal infection in immunocompromised patients
items.text: Treatment split: E. bieneusi → fumagillin. E. intestinalis → albendazole. Both → ART for immune reconstitution.

items.lo: Fungal intoxications affecting the GI tract
items.text: Costin verbatim: "they're never going to ask" about mycetismus gastrointestinalis on the exam. Recognize the term, know it's benign, move on.

items.lo: Fungal intoxications affecting the GI tract
items.text: Time-course clue: early-onset (<6 hours) + self-limited = mycetismus. Compare to delayed-onset (6-24h) = amatoxin (lethal).

items.lo: Fungal intoxications affecting the GI tract
items.text: Mechanism: α-amanitin irreversibly inhibits RNA polymerase II → halts mRNA synthesis → no new protein → cell death. Hepatocytes hit hardest (transcription-dependent); renal proximal tubule cells are second.

items.lo: Fungal intoxications affecting the GI tract
items.text: Chemical structure NOT testable per Costin. Know the mechanism (RNA pol II inhibition), not the chemistry.

items.lo: Fungal intoxications affecting the GI tract
items.text: Phase 1 (6-24h post-ingestion): nausea, severe vomiting, watery/bloody diarrhea, abdominal cramps. Looks like food poisoning.

items.lo: Fungal intoxications affecting the GI tract
items.text: Phase 2 (24-48h): APPARENT clinical improvement. GI symptoms abate. DANGER — this is when patients are discharged or feel reassured.

items.lo: Fungal intoxications affecting the GI tract
items.text: Phase 3 (days 3-5): fulminant hepatic failure — ALT/AST climb into the thousands, INR rises, jaundice, hepatic encephalopathy. Often accompanied by acute kidney injury.

items.lo: Fungal intoxications affecting the GI tract
items.text: Immediate management: activated charcoal (if <6h), aggressive IV fluids, identify mushroom, transfer to liver center. Do not discharge based on apparent improvement.

items.lo: Fungal intoxications affecting the GI tract
items.text: Source: Aspergillus flavus + A. parasiticus on improperly stored grains (corn, peanuts, nuts, oilseeds).

items.lo: Fungal intoxications affecting the GI tract
items.text: Isoforms (B1/B2/G1/G2) NOT testable per Costin. Know the mechanism + the HCC association.

items.topic: Mechanism — the most potent natural carcinogen known
items.lo: Fungal intoxications affecting the GI tract
items.text: Mechanism: CYP450 activates aflatoxin → epoxide → DNA adduct → p53 codon 249 mutation → HCC.

items.lo: Fungal intoxications affecting the GI tract
items.text: HBV synergy is the killer combination: HBV alone + aflatoxin alone each elevate HCC risk modestly. Together the risk multiplies (~30-60× over baseline).

items.lo: Fungal intoxications affecting the GI tract
items.text: Prevention: keep grains DRY and COOL. Aflatoxins are heat-stable; cooking won't save you.

items.lo: Fungal intoxications affecting the GI tract
items.text: Coprine = mushroom's Antabuse. Source: Coprinus atramentarius. Active metabolite: 1-aminocyclopropanol.

items.lo: Fungal intoxications affecting the GI tract
items.text: Mechanism = disulfiram-identical: coprine metabolite blocks ALDH → acetaldehyde accumulates when alcohol consumed → flush, tachycardia, nausea.

items.lo: Fungal intoxications affecting the GI tract
items.text: Counseling: avoid alcohol for ~3 days after eating Coprinus atramentarius. Outside that window, no symptoms.

items.lo: Fungal intoxications affecting the GI tract
items.text: Onset and resolution: 15-30 minutes after alcohol; self-limited in 2-3 hours.

items.lo: Fungal intoxications affecting the GI tract
items.text: Treatment: supportive (fluids, anti-emetics, monitor). No specific antidote. Self-limited.

los.statement: ●●○ Describe the role of the gastrointestinal system in digestion, absorption, and secretion
●●● Identify the composition and function of pancreatic, liver, and gallbladder secretions
●○○ Describe the role of the mucus layer and tight junctions in the gastrointestinal barrier

items.lo: ●●○ Describe the role of the gastrointestinal system in digestion, absorption, a
items.text: Why is the small intestine — not the colon — built for nutrient absorption, and what does that structural choice cost the colon?

items.topic: Two cell populations split the labor
items.lo: ●●○ Describe the role of the gastrointestinal system in digestion, absorption, a
items.text: Villi = small intestine only. Their job is absorption; the colon’s job is to dehydrate chyme (no villi in colon)

items.topic: Two cell populations split the labor
items.lo: ●●○ Describe the role of the gastrointestinal system in digestion, absorption, a
items.text: Absorptive enterocytes (villi) absorb; crypt enterocytes secrete (primary roles)

items.lo: ●●● Identify the composition and function of pancreatic, liver, and gallbladder
items.text: After a fatty meal, what single hormone simultaneously empties the gallbladder, opens the sphincter of Oddi, and revs the pancreas — and why does that same hormone show up again in the motility lecture?

items.lo: ●●● Identify the composition and function of pancreatic, liver, and gallbladder
items.text: CCK = I-cells → gallbladder contracts + sphincter of Oddi relaxes + pancreas secretes/grows (fat/protein trigger)

items.lo: ●●● Identify the composition and function of pancreatic, liver, and gallbladder
items.text: CCK also inhibits gastric emptying — a motility action (carries into Lec 5–6) (potent inhibitor)

items.lo: ●●● Identify the composition and function of pancreatic, liver, and gallbladder
items.text: Terminal ileum recycles ~95% of bile acids via enterohepatic circulation (and absorbs B12)

items.lo: ●●● Identify the composition and function of pancreatic, liver, and gallbladder
items.text: For each macronutrient, what is the rate-limiting enzyme, the apical transporter, and the exit door — and which single transporter do all three monosaccharides share on the way out?

items.lo: ●●● Identify the composition and function of pancreatic, liver, and gallbladder
items.text: SGLT1 = apical, Na-coupled glucose/galactose (intestine); SGLT2 = kidney (ORS basis)

items.lo: ●●● Identify the composition and function of pancreatic, liver, and gallbladder
items.text: GLUT5 = apical fructose (Na-independent); GLUT2 = basolateral exit for ALL three (classic trap = GLUT2 apical)

items.lo: ●●● Identify the composition and function of pancreatic, liver, and gallbladder
items.text: Lactose needs lactase (only); maltose has redundant enzymes (lactase deficiency → osmotic)

items.lo: ●●● Identify the composition and function of pancreatic, liver, and gallbladder
items.text: Enterokinase (brush border) activates trypsinogen → trypsin → all other proteases (duodenum only)

items.lo: ●●● Identify the composition and function of pancreatic, liver, and gallbladder
items.text: Cystinuria (COLA, cationic) + Hartnup (neutral, tryptophan) rescued via PepT1 dipeptides (Hartnup → pellagra)

items.topic: Lipid — emulsify, ferry, repackage, and route to lymph
items.lo: ●●● Identify the composition and function of pancreatic, liver, and gallbladder
items.text: Pancreatic lipase essential; bile acids emulsify; products ride micelles in (lingual/gastric minor)

items.topic: Lipid — emulsify, ferry, repackage, and route to lymph
items.lo: ●●● Identify the composition and function of pancreatic, liver, and gallbladder
items.text: Re-synthesized TAG → chylomicrons → lacteals (lymph, not portal) (MCFAs = portal exception)

items.lo: ●●● Identify the composition and function of pancreatic, liver, and gallbladder
items.text: The gut handles 8.5 L of fluid a day but loses only ~100 mL — where does all that fluid come from, and why is the jejunum (not the duodenum) the sodium workhorse?

items.lo: ●●● Identify the composition and function of pancreatic, liver, and gallbladder
items.text: 8.5 L presented/day, ~100 mL lost; ~6.5 L is GI secretion, ~2 L diet (colon gets ~2 L)

items.lo: ●●● Identify the composition and function of pancreatic, liver, and gallbladder
items.text: Na mechanisms: ENaC (colon, aldosterone) · Na-nutrient (duodenum) · Na-Cl · NHE — all electroneutral (Na/K-ATPase powers all)

items.lo: ●●● Identify the composition and function of pancreatic, liver, and gallbladder
items.text: Duodenum = nutrients + iron · Jejunum = major Na · Ileum = B12 + bile recycling (segment specialization)

items.topic: Iron — the duodenal function she would not let go of
items.lo: ●●● Identify the composition and function of pancreatic, liver, and gallbladder
items.text: Calcium: high intake = paracellular; low intake = transcellular (channel → calbindin → Ca-ATPase) under vit D3 (gut + vit D3)

items.topic: Iron — the duodenal function she would not let go of
items.lo: ●●● Identify the composition and function of pancreatic, liver, and gallbladder
items.text: Non-heme Fe order: Fe3+→Fe2+ by DCYTB (reductase) → DMT1 in → ferritin or FPN1 out (brush border = both)

items.lo: ●●● Identify the composition and function of pancreatic, liver, and gallbladder
items.text: Secretion uses the same chloride channel whether the trigger is cAMP or calcium — so how does cholera toxin turn a normal physiologic process into life-threatening diarrhea?

items.lo: ●●● Identify the composition and function of pancreatic, liver, and gallbladder
items.text: cAMP OR Ca2+ → opens apical Cl channel (CFTR) → Na follows → water follows (one endpoint)

items.lo: ●●● Identify the composition and function of pancreatic, liver, and gallbladder
items.text: cAMP: cholera, E. coli, VIP, histamine, prostaglandin · Ca2+: ACh, bradykinin, serotonin (sort by messenger)

items.lo: ●●● Identify the composition and function of pancreatic, liver, and gallbladder
items.text: Cholera/E. coli permanently raise cAMP → channel never closes → secretory diarrhea (persists when NPO)

items.lo: ●●● Identify the composition and function of pancreatic, liver, and gallbladder
items.text: Four diarrheas, four mechanisms — which one keeps going when the patient stops eating, and which lab pattern unmasks a destroyed villous surface?

items.lo: ●●● Identify the composition and function of pancreatic, liver, and gallbladder
items.text: Secretory persists with fasting (toxin/VIP) · Osmotic stops with fasting (lactose) (fasting test = key)

items.lo: ●●● Identify the composition and function of pancreatic, liver, and gallbladder
items.text: Inflammatory = lost surface area · Dysmotility = transit too fast (four mechanisms)

items.topic: Steatorrhea drags the fat-soluble vitamins down with it
items.lo: ●●● Identify the composition and function of pancreatic, liver, and gallbladder
items.text: Celiac = pan-malabsorption: steatorrhea + low d-xylose + hypoalbuminemic edema (surface-area loss)

items.topic: Steatorrhea drags the fat-soluble vitamins down with it
items.lo: ●●● Identify the composition and function of pancreatic, liver, and gallbladder
items.text: Steatorrhea → lose A, D, E, K; B12 deficiency is an ileum/IF problem (water-soluble spared)

los.statement: ●●○ Identify functional subdivisions of the GI tract and their roles in motility

items.lo: ●●○ Identify functional subdivisions of the GI tract and their roles in motility
items.text: If peristalsis is the one pattern that actually moves food, what do segmentation, mass movement, and haustration add that peristalsis alone cannot?

items.topic: Segment specialization is the testable spine
items.lo: ●●○ Identify functional subdivisions of the GI tract and their roles in motility
items.text: Peristalsis propels (contract behind / relax ahead); segmentation mixes; mass movement is the colonic propulsive surge (peristalsis = dominant)

items.topic: Segment specialization is the testable spine
items.lo: ●●○ Identify functional subdivisions of the GI tract and their roles in motility
items.text: Haustration = the colon’s peristalsis variant, named for the haustra (pockets) (colon-specific)

items.lo: ●●○ Identify functional subdivisions of the GI tract and their roles in motility
items.text: Trace a single swallow from voluntary to involuntary, and explain why a solid meal leaves the stomach with a lag phase that a glass of water does not.

items.topic: ESOPHAGEAL PERISTALSIS — one third striated, two thirds smooth
items.lo: ●●○ Identify functional subdivisions of the GI tract and their roles in motility
items.text: Voluntary = oral prep + oral stage; involuntary begins at the pharyngeal swallow (switch at back of mouth)

items.topic: ESOPHAGEAL PERISTALSIS — one third striated, two thirds smooth
items.lo: ●●○ Identify functional subdivisions of the GI tract and their roles in motility
items.text: Esophagus = upper 1/3 striated (myasthenia gravis) + lower 2/3 smooth (scleroderma) (disease maps to muscle type)

items.topic: ESOPHAGEAL PERISTALSIS — one third striated, two thirds smooth
items.lo: ●●○ Identify functional subdivisions of the GI tract and their roles in motility
items.text: Primary peristalsis = swallow-initiated; secondary = ENS mop-up of residue ("food stuck in chest")

items.topic: The 2-mm rule and the solid-meal lag phase
items.lo: ●●○ Identify functional subdivisions of the GI tract and their roles in motility
items.text: Orad/fundus = accommodation (near-threshold, tonic); corpus = mixing; antrum = emptying (region = pattern)

items.topic: The 2-mm rule and the solid-meal lag phase
items.lo: ●●○ Identify functional subdivisions of the GI tract and their roles in motility
items.text: Pylorus excludes particles > 2 mm → antral grinding → solid-meal lag phase (liquids = no lag)

items.lo: ●●○ Identify functional subdivisions of the GI tract and their roles in motility
items.text: If slow waves never cause a contraction by themselves, what are they for — and what turns a slow wave into an actual contraction?

items.topic: WHY THE NERVOUS SYSTEM IS STILL REQUIRED
items.lo: ●●○ Identify functional subdivisions of the GI tract and their roles in motility
items.text: Slow waves = regular sub-threshold oscillations, NOT action potentials, neurally/hormonally uninfluenced (pacemaking only)

items.topic: WHY THE NERVOUS SYSTEM IS STILL REQUIRED
items.lo: ●●○ Identify functional subdivisions of the GI tract and their roles in motility
items.text: Spike potential = true AP at the slow-wave crest once threshold (~−40 mV) is met (ACh sensitizes)

items.topic: WHY THE NERVOUS SYSTEM IS STILL REQUIRED
items.lo: ●●○ Identify functional subdivisions of the GI tract and their roles in motility
items.text: ICCs generate slow waves; the ICC + smooth-muscle unit is required for contraction (lost in gastroparesis)

items.lo: ●●○ Identify functional subdivisions of the GI tract and their roles in motility
items.text: Why does your stomach growl when you skip a meal, and what stops that pattern the moment you eat — but only if you eat, not if you are fed intravenously?

items.lo: ●●○ Identify functional subdivisions of the GI tract and their roles in motility
items.text: MMC = fasting-only housekeeper, every ~90 min, keeps small bowel sterile; reduced → SIBO (IBS lowers it)

items.lo: ●●○ Identify functional subdivisions of the GI tract and their roles in motility
items.text: MMC stops with enteral nutrients (not IV); driven entirely by the ENS (no vagovagal) (physical presence of food)

items.lo: ●●○ Identify functional subdivisions of the GI tract and their roles in motility
items.text: Motilin stimulates MMC + gastric emptying; CCK inhibits emptying (opposite hormones)

items.lo: ●●○ Identify functional subdivisions of the GI tract and their roles in motility
items.text: Vomiting uses the same machinery as normal propulsion — so what single change turns orderly forward motility into expulsion, and what triggers it?

items.lo: ●●○ Identify functional subdivisions of the GI tract and their roles in motility
items.text: Vomiting = a retrograde antiperistaltic wave out through LES → UES → mouth (reversed peristalsis)

items.lo: ●●○ Identify functional subdivisions of the GI tract and their roles in motility
items.text: CRTZ (chemoreceptor trigger zone) initiates emesis; chemotherapy is the classic trigger (irritant-driven)

items.lo: ●●○ Identify functional subdivisions of the GI tract and their roles in motility
items.text: In the smooth-muscle esophagus, why must an inhibitory signal fire BEFORE the excitatory one — and how is one GI hormone able to coordinate accommodation and emptying at once?

items.topic: HORMONES — and CCK’s elegant double action
items.lo: ●●○ Identify functional subdivisions of the GI tract and their roles in motility
items.text: ACh (excitatory) contracts; NO/VIP (inhibitory) relaxes — inhibition fires first, ahead of the bolus (lower esophagus = more inhibitory)

items.topic: HORMONES — and CCK’s elegant double action
items.lo: ●●○ Identify functional subdivisions of the GI tract and their roles in motility
items.text: Vagovagal: accommodation + pancreatic secretion. Enteric-only: the MMC (no vagovagal) (control-system split)

items.topic: HORMONES — and CCK’s elegant double action
items.lo: ●●○ Identify functional subdivisions of the GI tract and their roles in motility
items.text: CCK inhibits gastric emptying AND promotes orad accommodation (one hormone, two actions)

items.lo: ●●○ Identify functional subdivisions of the GI tract and their roles in motility
items.text: Achalasia, gastroparesis, Hirschsprung, and fecal incontinence each break a different link in the motility chain — which link, and what is the tell for each?

items.lo: ●●○ Identify functional subdivisions of the GI tract and their roles in motility
items.text: Achalasia = LES won’t relax + lost peristalsis → dysphagia to solids AND liquids, bird-beak (neurogenic)

items.lo: ●●○ Identify functional subdivisions of the GI tract and their roles in motility
items.text: Gastroparesis = disorganized emptying, diabetes → ICC loss; pylorectomy → dumping (opposite extremes)

items.lo: ●●○ Identify functional subdivisions of the GI tract and their roles in motility
items.text: Hirschsprung = aganglionic distal bowel; continence needs the voluntary external anal sphincter (ENS migration failure)

los.statement: ●●○ Identify functional subdivisions of the GI tract and their roles in motility

items.lo: ●●○ Identify functional subdivisions of the GI tract and their roles in motility
items.text: If peristalsis is the one pattern that actually moves food, what do segmentation, mass movement, and haustration add that peristalsis alone cannot?

items.topic: Segment specialization is the testable spine
items.lo: ●●○ Identify functional subdivisions of the GI tract and their roles in motility
items.text: Peristalsis propels (contract behind / relax ahead); segmentation mixes; mass movement is the colonic propulsive surge (peristalsis = dominant)

items.topic: Segment specialization is the testable spine
items.lo: ●●○ Identify functional subdivisions of the GI tract and their roles in motility
items.text: Haustration = the colon’s peristalsis variant, named for the haustra (pockets) (colon-specific)
