los.statement: ●●● Identify the function of the lower esophageal sphincter (LES) in the development of GERD
●●○ Describe the various stages of disease severity in GERD
●●○ Discuss the symptomatology associated with GERD
●●● Describe how GERD is diagnosed and the role of endoscopy

items.lo: ●●● Identify the function of the lower esophageal sphincter (LES) in the develop
items.text: Reflux is just fluid moving the wrong way — so in pure pressure terms, what has to be true at the lower esophageal sphincter for stomach acid to climb back into the esophagus?

items.lo: ●●● Identify the function of the lower esophageal sphincter (LES) in the develop
items.text: Normal LES baseline = 10–35 mmHg; incompetent LES < 10 mmHg (pressure threshold)

items.lo: ●●● Identify the function of the lower esophageal sphincter (LES) in the develop
items.text: Reflux = incompetent LES + intragastric pressure > esophageal pressure (gradient drives flow)

items.lo: ●●● Identify the function of the lower esophageal sphincter (LES) in the develop
items.text: Anti-reflux barrier = LES + crural diaphragm (the external sphincter) (two structures)

items.lo: ●●● Identify the function of the lower esophageal sphincter (LES) in the develop
items.text: Hiatal hernia in ~1/4 non-erosive but ~3/4 severe esophagitis; pH < 4 erodes mucosa (hernia severity link)

items.lo: ●●○ Describe the various stages of disease severity in GERD
items.text: Two patients both have reflux on prescription PPIs — one is controlled, one is not. Which stage is each, and why does that distinction change management?

items.lo: ●●○ Describe the various stages of disease severity in GERD
items.text: Stage 1 = 1–2×/month, lifestyle + OTC; Stage 2 = daily Rx, controlled (frequency + treatment)

items.lo: ●●○ Describe the various stages of disease severity in GERD
items.text: Stage 3 = uncontrolled on Rx + highly erosive; Stage 4 = reflux-induced carcinoma (control is the hinge)

items.lo: ●●○ Discuss the symptomatology associated with GERD
items.text: Most GERD looks like heartburn after meals — but a patient with a months-long cough and no lung disease turns out to have reflux. How does GERD hide?

items.lo: ●●○ Discuss the symptomatology associated with GERD
items.text: Typical GERD = heartburn + regurgitation + sour/bitter taste, 30–60 min postprandial, worse reclining (timing is the tell)

items.lo: ●●○ Discuss the symptomatology associated with GERD
items.text: Atypical/extraesophageal = cough, hoarseness, asthma, throat clearing; clinical dx ~65% (GERD in disguise)

items.lo: ●●○ Discuss the symptomatology associated with GERD
items.text: H. pylori DECREASES GERD risk (less acid) but → MALT/gastric cancer; eradication can worsen GERD (counterintuitive)

items.lo: ●●● Describe how GERD is diagnosed and the role of endoscopy
items.text: If uncomplicated GERD needs no testing at all, what specific findings flip a patient from "just treat empirically" to "scope them now"?

items.lo: ●●● Describe how GERD is diagnosed and the role of endoscopy
items.text: Uncomplicated GERD → empiric acid suppression, no testing; response ~78% sens / 54% spec (treat first)

items.lo: ●●● Describe how GERD is diagnosed and the role of endoscopy
items.text: Alarm features = dysphagia, odynophagia, weight loss, iron-deficiency anemia → scope (when to endoscope)

items.lo: ●●● Describe how GERD is diagnosed and the role of endoscopy
items.text: Scope before acid suppression — therapy heals and obscures esophagitis (timing of endoscopy)

items.lo: ●●● Describe how GERD is diagnosed and the role of endoscopy
items.text: Fundoplication = 360° fundic wrap recreating the LES; heals esophagitis >85% (surgical option)

los.statement: ●●○ Outline the principles of management of bacterial infections of the gastrointestinal system
●●○ Outline the principles of management of virus infections of the gastrointestinal system

items.lo: ●●○ Outline the principles of management of bacterial infections of the gastroin
items.text: If almost every one of these infections is managed the same way, what is that default — and which organism is the one where the "obvious" instinct to give antibiotics is actually wrong?

items.lo: ●●○ Outline the principles of management of bacterial infections of the gastroin
items.text: Default management for bacterial gastroenteritis = fluid & electrolyte repletion (most are self-limited)

items.lo: ●●○ Outline the principles of management of bacterial infections of the gastroin
items.text: Cholera → aggressive rehydration is lifesaving (massive secretory loss → shock) (volume is the threat)

items.lo: ●●○ Outline the principles of management of bacterial infections of the gastroin
items.text: EHEC → avoid antibiotics (no benefit, increased HUS risk); fluids only (management trap)

items.lo: ●●○ Outline the principles of management of bacterial infections of the gastroin
items.text: Three viruses all cause watery diarrhea — so which genome, which population, and which classic setting separates rotavirus, norovirus, and adenovirus?

items.lo: ●●○ Outline the principles of management of bacterial infections of the gastroin
items.text: Rotavirus = dsRNA, children, winter; vomiting precedes diarrhea; NSP4 osmotic (pediatric, vaccine exists)

items.lo: ●●○ Outline the principles of management of bacterial infections of the gastroin
items.text: Norovirus = ssRNA, adults, cruise-ship/institutional outbreaks; HBGA binding (no vaccine)

items.lo: ●●○ Outline the principles of management of bacterial infections of the gastroin
items.text: Adenovirus = dsDNA, serotypes 40/41, children/immunocompromised (only DNA virus here)

items.lo: ●●○ Outline the principles of management of bacterial infections of the gastroin
items.text: Most sensitive viral test = RT-PCR; HBGA enhances rotavirus + norovirus binding (shared binding factor)

items.lo: ●●○ Outline the principles of management of virus infections of the gastrointest
items.text: Viral gastroenteritis has no routine antiviral — so what actually constitutes "management," and what is the one preventive tool that exists for only one of these viruses?

items.lo: ●●○ Outline the principles of management of virus infections of the gastrointest
items.text: Viral gastroenteritis management = supportive fluids & electrolytes; no routine antivirals (self-limited)

items.lo: ●●○ Outline the principles of management of virus infections of the gastrointest
items.text: Rotavirus has a live attenuated vaccine; norovirus & adenovirus do NOT (only rotavirus is vaccine-preventable)

los.statement: ●●○ Define the energy-metabolism vocabulary
●●● Role of the liver in energy storage and transfer
●●○ Relationship of carbohydrate, fat, and protein metabolism
●●○ Endocrine and neurocrine factors regulating food intake
●●○ GI hormones and the liver in feeding and fasting energy homeostasis
●○○ Neurotransmitters and chemical stimuli regulating GI secretions
●●○ Nutrients, intestinal hormones, and the ANS regulating insulin and glucagon secretion
●●● Contrast insulin and glucagon on the liver, feeding versus fasting

items.lo: ●●○ Define the energy-metabolism vocabulary
items.text: If a term ends in “-lysis” versus “-genesis,” which direction of the reaction is it, and why does that suffix tell you the answer before you know any biochemistry?

items.lo: ●●○ Define the energy-metabolism vocabulary
items.text: Respiratory quotient = volume CO2 released ÷ volume O2 absorbed. (RQ definition)

items.lo: ●●○ Define the energy-metabolism vocabulary
items.text: Basal metabolic rate = calories needed for basic life-sustaining function; differs per person. (BMR definition)

items.lo: ●●○ Define the energy-metabolism vocabulary
items.text: Aerobic metabolism = the oxygen-using TCA pathway; glycolysis alone is the anaerobic front end. (aerobic vs glycolysis)

items.lo: ●●● Role of the liver in energy storage and transfer
items.text: You are deep into a fast with glycogen nearly gone but blood glucose still defended. Which liver pathways are running, and where is the glucose actually coming from?

items.lo: ●●● Role of the liver in energy storage and transfer
items.text: Glycogenesis stores excess glucose as glycogen when blood glucose is high; glycogenolysis reverses it. (storage pair direction)

items.lo: ●●● Role of the liver in energy storage and transfer
items.text: Pyruvate dehydrogenase converts pyruvate → acetyl-CoA, the convergence molecule for the TCA cycle. (PDH product)

items.lo: ●●● Role of the liver in energy storage and transfer
items.text: Ketogenesis fires only when glucose AND glycogen are both depleted. (ketogenesis trigger)

items.lo: ●●● Role of the liver in energy storage and transfer
items.text: Excess carbohydrate beyond glycogen capacity → lipogenesis → stored triglyceride. (carb surplus fate)

items.lo: ●●○ Relationship of carbohydrate, fat, and protein metabolism
items.text: Two hepatocytes differ only in their ATP:AMP ratio. Without knowing anything else, what can you predict about which pathways each one is running?

items.lo: ●●○ Relationship of carbohydrate, fat, and protein metabolism
items.text: High ATP:AMP → glycolysis inhibited, storage (glycogenesis + lipogenesis) up; low ATP:AMP → breakdown up. (ATP ratio controls pathway)

items.lo: ●●○ Endocrine and neurocrine factors regulating food intake
items.text: Your stomach is empty and signaling hunger. Trace the hormone from the stomach to the hypothalamic peptide it releases, and name the appetite-suppressing signal it shuts off.

items.lo: ●●○ Endocrine and neurocrine factors regulating food intake
items.text: Ghrelin (empty stomach) → hypothalamic NPY/AgRP release → hunger; it also inhibits alpha-MSH. (ghrelin hunger axis)

items.lo: ●●○ Endocrine and neurocrine factors regulating food intake
items.text: Orexigenic = NPY, AgRP, ghrelin, cortisol; anorexigenic = alpha-MSH, insulin, leptin, GLP-1, CCK, serotonin. (appetite hormone sort)

items.lo: ●●○ GI hormones and the liver in feeding and fasting energy homeostasis
items.text: During an overnight fast the brain still gets its glucose. Which four hormones make that happen, and what is the one organ they are all leaning on?

items.lo: ●●○ GI hormones and the liver in feeding and fasting energy homeostasis
items.text: Fasting brain-glucose defenders: glucagon, epinephrine, cortisol, growth hormone. (fasting counter-regulatory set)

items.lo: ●●○ GI hormones and the liver in feeding and fasting energy homeostasis
items.text: GLP-1 and CCK are both fed-state digestive hormones AND anorexigenic satiety signals. (gut hormone dual role)

items.lo: ●○○ Neurotransmitters and chemical stimuli regulating GI secretions
items.text: Secretin → bicarbonate (neutralizes chyme); GLP-1 inhibits secretin so acid is preserved for pepsin activation. (secretin role)

items.lo: ●●○ Nutrients, intestinal hormones, and the ANS regulating insulin and glucagon
items.text: A protein-rich meal raises both insulin and glucagon, yet a carbohydrate meal raises insulin but suppresses glucagon. Why does the body want glucagon up after a steak?

items.lo: ●●○ Nutrients, intestinal hormones, and the ANS regulating insulin and glucagon
items.text: Insulin stimulated by hyperglycemia/amino acids/GIP; inhibited by glucagon/hypoglycemia/somatostatin/exercise/leptin. (insulin regulators)

items.lo: ●●○ Nutrients, intestinal hormones, and the ANS regulating insulin and glucagon
items.text: Glucagon stimulated by hypoglycemia/protein meal/stress/exercise; inhibited by hyperglycemia/carbs/GLP-1. (glucagon regulators)

items.lo: ●●○ Nutrients, intestinal hormones, and the ANS regulating insulin and glucagon
items.text: Amino acids stimulate BOTH insulin and glucagon; the glucagon rise prevents hypoglycemia after a low-carb protein meal. (amino-acid dual stimulation)

items.lo: ●●● Contrast insulin and glucagon on the liver, feeding versus fasting
items.text: Given only that someone just finished a large pasta dinner, predict the dominant pancreatic hormone, the liver’s main action, and where the absorbed glucose ends up.

items.lo: ●●● Contrast insulin and glucagon on the liver, feeding versus fasting
items.text: Fasting: glucose ↓ → insulin ↓ → glucagon ↑ → liver runs glycogenolysis + gluconeogenesis to export glucose. (fasting chain)

items.lo: ●●● Contrast insulin and glucagon on the liver, feeding versus fasting
items.text: Feeding: glucose ↑ → insulin ↑ → glycogenesis + lipogenesis store fuel; surplus glucose → triglyceride. (feeding chain)

items.lo: ●●● Contrast insulin and glucagon on the liver, feeding versus fasting
items.text: Insulin:glucagon ratio ≈ 2:1 balanced, 10:1 after carbs, 1:2 fasting/stress. (insulin:glucagon ratio)

items.lo: ●●● Contrast insulin and glucagon on the liver, feeding versus fasting
items.text: Insulin and glucagon are never both high — each inhibits the other (reciprocal control). (reciprocal rule)

los.statement: ●●○ Pathologic features of selected oral inflammatory lesions
●●○ Morphology and clinical aspects of oral premalignant lesions
●●● Etiology, morphology, and clinical aspects of oral tumors
●●○ Major pathologic features of tumors of the jaw
●●● Major inflammatory lesions and tumors of the salivary glands

items.lo: ●●○ Pathologic features of selected oral inflammatory lesions
items.text: Two patients each have a white or ulcerated oral lesion — one wipes off with gauze, one does not, and a third sits only on non-keratinized mucosa. How does surface preference plus the wipe-off test sort the inflammatory lesions?

items.lo: ●●○ Pathologic features of selected oral inflammatory lesions
items.text: Aphthous ulcers sit almost exclusively on non-keratinized mucosa; recurrent HSV favors keratinized mucosa. (aphthous vs HSV surface)

items.lo: ●●○ Pathologic features of selected oral inflammatory lesions
items.text: Aphthous ulcers >1 cm are the least common and are seen in AIDS patients. (large aphthae = AIDS)

items.lo: ●●○ Pathologic features of selected oral inflammatory lesions
items.text: Thrush wipes off (pseudomembranous); leukoplakia and hyperplastic candidiasis do NOT. (wipe-off test)

items.lo: ●●○ Pathologic features of selected oral inflammatory lesions
items.text: Erythematous candidiasis = “antibiotic sore mouth,” dorsal tongue + angular cheilitis. (antibiotic sore mouth)

items.lo: ●●○ Pathologic features of selected oral inflammatory lesions
items.text: Pyogenic granuloma = exophytic immature vessels, gingiva of pregnancy (“pregnancy tumor”); excise. (pregnancy tumor)

items.lo: ●●○ Morphology and clinical aspects of oral premalignant lesions
items.text: Two premalignant patches sit side by side — one white and sharply demarcated, one red and velvety. Which carries the higher malignant risk, and what single factor most drives both?

items.lo: ●●○ Morphology and clinical aspects of oral premalignant lesions
items.text: Erythroplakia (red) > leukoplakia (white) for malignant risk: >50% transform vs 5–25% dysplastic. (red beats white for risk)

items.lo: ●●○ Morphology and clinical aspects of oral premalignant lesions
items.text: Both premalignant lesions: most commonly associated with tobacco; 40–70 yo, M:F ~2:1. (shared tobacco risk)

items.lo: ●●● Etiology, morphology, and clinical aspects of oral tumors
items.text: Two oral squamous cell carcinomas — one keratinizing with adjacent leukoplakia in an older smoker, one non-keratinizing in a younger patient with cervical nodes. How do HPV status, molecular driver, morphology, and metastatic behavior separate them?

items.lo: ●●● Etiology, morphology, and clinical aspects of oral tumors
items.text: ~95% of oral cancers are SCC; most common site = ventral tongue. (oral SCC frequency + site)

items.lo: ●●● Etiology, morphology, and clinical aspects of oral tumors
items.text: HPV-16 → oropharyngeal SCC; tobacco+alcohol → oral cavity; actinic/pipe → lower lip. (cause tracks location)

items.lo: ●●● Etiology, morphology, and clinical aspects of oral tumors
items.text: HPV-negative driver = TP53/CDKN2A (+ field cancerization); HPV-positive driver = E6→p53, E7→Rb. (HPV molecular split)

items.lo: ●●● Etiology, morphology, and clinical aspects of oral tumors
items.text: HPV-negative = keratinizing + keratin pearls + precursor lesion; HPV-positive = non-keratinizing basaloid + p16 + cervical nodes, no precursor.

items.lo: ●●○ Major pathologic features of tumors of the jaw
items.text: Three jaw cysts: one around an unerupted tooth crown, one at a root tip, one keratinized with parakeratosis. How does the epithelial lining plus the location sort them, and which one is aggressive?

items.lo: ●●○ Major pathologic features of tumors of the jaw
items.text: Dentigerous = unerupted-tooth crown, non-keratinized; OKC = keratinized + parakeratosis, aggressive; periapical = root tip, inflammatory, most common.

items.lo: ●●○ Major pathologic features of tumors of the jaw
items.text: Ameloblastoma = soap-bubble multilocular, molar-ramus mandible, palisading nuclei, locally invasive; odontoma = most common, hamartoma. (two odontogenic tumors)

items.lo: ●●● Major inflammatory lesions and tumors of the salivary glands
items.text: Three salivary gland neoplasms come across your desk — the most common overall, the smoker’s tumor with germinal centers, and the most common malignant one. How do frequency, capsule, and histology separate them?

items.lo: ●●● Major inflammatory lesions and tumors of the salivary glands
items.text: Mucocele = fluctuant lower-lip lesion that changes size with meals (blocked/ruptured duct). (mucocele tell)

items.lo: ●●● Major inflammatory lesions and tumors of the salivary glands
items.text: Mumps (Rubulavirus) = most common viral sialadenitis; most common cause of postnatal-viral deafness. (mumps deafness)

items.lo: ●●● Major inflammatory lesions and tumors of the salivary glands
items.text: Salivary neoplasm ranking: pleomorphic adenoma (most common overall, benign) > Warthin (2nd, smokers) > mucoepidermoid (most common malignant). (neoplasm ranking)

items.lo: ●●● Major inflammatory lesions and tumors of the salivary glands
items.text: Warthin tell = germinal centers + double oncocytic layer; pleomorphic = chondromyxoid stroma. (salivary histology tells)

los.statement: ●●○ Pathologic features of congenital anomalies of the esophagus
●●○ Motor dysfunction: achalasia, hiatal hernia, diverticula, Mallory-Weiss
●●● Etiology, pathogenesis, and morphology of esophagitis and Barrett esophagus
●●○ Etiology and pathological features of esophageal varices
●●● Predisposing factors, types, morphology, and prognosis of esophageal carcinoma

items.lo: ●●○ Pathologic features of congenital anomalies of the esophagus
items.text: A newborn cannot pass food to the stomach, and a middle-aged woman has webs, anemia, and dysphagia. What is the most common congenital esophageal anomaly, and what is that adult triad?

items.lo: ●●○ Pathologic features of congenital anomalies of the esophagus
items.text: Most common congenital esophageal anomaly = tracheoesophageal fistula (usually with atresia), ~1 in 3,500. (most common anomaly)

items.lo: ●●○ Pathologic features of congenital anomalies of the esophagus
items.text: Plummer-Vinson = webs + iron-deficiency anemia + dysphagia; ↑carcinoma risk. Webs = anywhere; Schatzki ring = distal, at GE junction. (web triad vs Schatzki)

items.lo: ●●○ Motor dysfunction: achalasia, hiatal hernia, diverticula, Mallory-Weiss
items.text: One esophagus will not relax its LES; one herniates the stomach through the diaphragm; one pouches above the UES; one tears after vomiting. How do you sort these four motor problems — and which tear becomes a surgical emergency?

items.lo: ●●○ Motor dysfunction: achalasia, hiatal hernia, diverticula, Mallory-Weiss
items.text: Achalasia = ↑LES tone + no relaxation + no peristalsis (bird-beak); dysphagia for solids AND liquids. GERD = ↓LES tone. (achalasia vs GERD tone)

items.lo: ●●○ Motor dysfunction: achalasia, hiatal hernia, diverticula, Mallory-Weiss
items.text: Secondary achalasia → Chagas disease (T. cruzi destroys the myenteric plexus). (Chagas achalasia)

items.lo: ●●○ Motor dysfunction: achalasia, hiatal hernia, diverticula, Mallory-Weiss
items.text: Sliding (~85%) = GE junction slides up; paraesophageal = fundus herniates, GE junction often normal; type IV → surgery (volvulus/ischemia). (sliding vs paraesophageal)

items.lo: ●●○ Motor dysfunction: achalasia, hiatal hernia, diverticula, Mallory-Weiss
items.text: Mallory-Weiss = MUCOSAL tear (painful hematemesis after vomiting); Boerhaave = FULL-THICKNESS rupture → mediastinitis. (Mallory-Weiss vs Boerhaave)

items.lo: ●●○ Motor dysfunction: achalasia, hiatal hernia, diverticula, Mallory-Weiss
items.text: Zenker = above UES; traction = mid; epiphrenic = above LES. True = all layers; false = mucosa+submucosa. (diverticulum locations)

items.lo: ●●● Etiology, pathogenesis, and morphology of esophagitis and Barrett esophagus
items.text: Four esophagitis pictures: acid-driven streaks, allergic rings with eosinophils, punched-out viral ulcers, and salmon mucosa with goblet cells. How do you sort the esophagitides, and which one is the famous premalignant metaplasia?

items.lo: ●●● Etiology, pathogenesis, and morphology of esophagitis and Barrett esophagus
items.text: GERD micro = basal zone hyperplasia + elongated papillae + inflammatory cells; macro = vertical linear erosions. (GERD histology)

items.lo: ●●● Etiology, pathogenesis, and morphology of esophagitis and Barrett esophagus
items.text: Eosinophilic dyad: concentric rings (trachealization) + >15 eosinophils/HPF; biopsy MULTIPLE levels (patchy). (eosinophilic high-yield dyad)

items.lo: ●●● Etiology, pathogenesis, and morphology of esophagitis and Barrett esophagus
items.text: HSV = nuclear inclusions in squamous cells (punched-out ulcers); CMV = inclusions in endothelial/fibroblast cells, NOT squamous. (HSV vs CMV cell type)

items.lo: ●●● Etiology, pathogenesis, and morphology of esophagitis and Barrett esophagus
items.text: Barrett = squamous→columnar metaplasia (distal third, chronic GERD); intestinal metaplasia with goblet cells = most common + pathognomonic → ↑adenocarcinoma. (Barrett pathognomonic)

items.lo: ●●○ Etiology and pathological features of esophageal varices
items.text: A cirrhotic patient bleeds massively from the distal esophagus. What vascular lesion is this, what drives it, and how does it differ from a vomiting-induced tear?

items.lo: ●●○ Etiology and pathological features of esophageal varices
items.text: Varices = dilated distal submucosal veins (portal HTN/cirrhosis), beneath intact mucosa; bleed massively. Contrast Mallory-Weiss (mucosal tear). (varices vs Mallory-Weiss)

items.lo: ●●● Predisposing factors, types, morphology, and prognosis of esophageal carcino
items.text: Two esophageal cancers: one distal in an obese GERD patient with adjacent goblet cells, one mid-esophageal in a heavy drinker/smoker. How do you separate adenocarcinoma from squamous cell carcinoma, and what determines prognosis?

items.lo: ●●● Predisposing factors, types, morphology, and prognosis of esophageal carcino
items.text: Adenocarcinoma = N. America/Europe, Barrett/GERD, distal third, Caucasian males ~7:1; arises via p53 from Barrett. (adenocarcinoma profile)

items.lo: ●●● Predisposing factors, types, morphology, and prognosis of esophageal carcino
items.text: SCC = worldwide (~90%), alcohol+tobacco (synergistic), upper/middle third; gray-white plaque → invasive. (SCC profile)

items.topic: Staging by depth of invasion + lymph nodes
items.lo: ●●● Predisposing factors, types, morphology, and prognosis of esophageal carcino
items.text: Prognosis depends on depth of invasion + lymph nodes (Stage 0–IV); late diagnosis → 5-yr survival <25% (adeno). (staging drives prognosis)

los.statement: ●●○ Esophageal hiatal hernia: anatomic type and need for treatment
●●● Anatomic/physiologic factors predisposing to reflux; symptoms and diagnosis
●●● Operative management of reflux; achalasia pathophysiology and management; diverticula
●●● Esophageal malignancy: symptoms, diagnostic plan, natural history, treatment
●●○ Benign neoplasm; traumatic perforation; diaphragmatic hernia; caustic ingestion

items.lo: ●●○ Esophageal hiatal hernia: anatomic type and need for treatment
items.text: Two hiatal hernias: one common and often silent, one that stays quiet until it strangulates. How do you tell them apart, and which one gets repaired even when mild?

items.lo: ●●○ Esophageal hiatal hernia: anatomic type and need for treatment
items.text: Sliding (type I) = ~100x more common, GE junction slides up, often asymptomatic; pain does NOT radiate. (sliding hernia)

items.lo: ●●○ Esophageal hiatal hernia: anatomic type and need for treatment
items.text: Paraesophageal = fundus herniates, silent until incarceration/ischemia; type IV often repaired to avoid volvulus/gastric ischemia. (paraesophageal danger)

items.lo: ●●● Anatomic/physiologic factors predisposing to reflux; symptoms and diagnosis
items.text: What physiologic factors let acid reflux happen, what makes the symptoms surgical rather than cardiac, and which test confirms which part of the picture?

items.lo: ●●● Anatomic/physiologic factors predisposing to reflux; symptoms and diagnosis
items.text: Reflux injury = function of acid-contact DURATION, not hyperacidity; GE junction is a pressure zone, not a true sphincter. (duration not acidity)

items.lo: ●●● Anatomic/physiologic factors predisposing to reflux; symptoms and diagnosis
items.text: Reflux symptoms worse supine, relieved by antacids; late dysphagia/vomiting → stricture. (reflux symptom pattern)

items.lo: ●●● Anatomic/physiologic factors predisposing to reflux; symptoms and diagnosis
items.text: Dx triad: barium = anatomy, endoscopy+biopsy = mucosa, manometry = physiology (lost high-pressure zone). (diagnostic triad)

items.lo: ●●● Operative management of reflux; achalasia pathophysiology and management; di
items.text: When does reflux earn an operation and what does that operation actually do? Why is achalasia painless despite a hugely dilated esophagus? And how do a Zenker and a mid-esophageal diverticulum differ in mechanism?

items.lo: ●●● Operative management of reflux; achalasia pathophysiology and management; di
items.text: Operative reflux: ~1/3 fail medical tx, half of responders relapse; operation = correct defect + rebuild a valve (principle, not named wrap). (when reflux goes to surgery)

items.lo: ●●● Operative management of reflux; achalasia pathophysiology and management; di
items.text: Antireflux complication = gas-bloat syndrome (cannot belch/vomit); also dysphagia, recurrence, perforation, splenic injury. (gas-bloat complication)

items.lo: ●●● Operative management of reflux; achalasia pathophysiology and management; di
items.text: Achalasia = failure to relax (NOT spasm) + progressive proximal dilation; painless because the dilation is non-ischemic. (why achalasia is painless)

items.lo: ●●● Operative management of reflux; achalasia pathophysiology and management; di
items.text: Bird-beak on upper GI = achalasia (board-flagged, "do not miss"); confirmed by contrast → endoscopy → manometry. (bird-beak board question)

items.lo: ●●● Operative management of reflux; achalasia pathophysiology and management; di
items.text: Achalasia tx: medical not helpful → forceful dilation or esophageal myotomy. (achalasia treatment)

items.lo: ●●● Operative management of reflux; achalasia pathophysiology and management; di
items.text: Pulsion = Zenker (cervical, cricopharyngeal dysfunction; excise + myotomy) & distal-third (fix the cause). Traction = mid-esophageal (TB/histoplasmosis; usually no tx). (pulsion vs traction)

items.lo: ●●● Esophageal malignancy: symptoms, diagnostic plan, natural history, treatment
items.text: A patient has progressive dysphagia and weight loss. How do you recognize whether it is squamous cell or adenocarcinoma, how do you stage it, and how does location decide treatment?

items.lo: ●●● Esophageal malignancy: symptoms, diagnostic plan, natural history, treatment
items.text: SCC = squamous epithelium, black men, alcohol/tobacco. Adeno = ~10% of Barrett (columnar change). (SCC vs adeno risk)

items.lo: ●●● Esophageal malignancy: symptoms, diagnostic plan, natural history, treatment
items.text: Insidious onset → progressive dysphagia; stage with EUS (depth) + CT chest/abdomen (extent). (staging workup)

items.lo: ●●● Esophageal malignancy: symptoms, diagnostic plan, natural history, treatment
items.text: Tx by location: upper → radiotherapy; middle → staged esophagectomy + bypass; lower/proximal stomach → esophagogastric resection + end-to-end anastomosis. (treatment by location)

items.lo: ●●○ Benign neoplasm; traumatic perforation; diaphragmatic hernia; caustic ingest
items.text: A rare intramural tumor, a spontaneous esophageal rupture after vomiting, and a child who swallowed drain cleaner. How is each recognized and managed — and what must you NOT do for the caustic ingestion?

items.lo: ●●○ Benign neoplasm; traumatic perforation; diaphragmatic hernia; caustic ingest
items.text: Leiomyoma = most common benign intramural tumor; low malignant potential; indents lumen; progressive dysphagia. (leiomyoma)

items.lo: ●●○ Benign neoplasm; traumatic perforation; diaphragmatic hernia; caustic ingest
items.text: Boerhaave = spontaneous full-thickness perforation from vomiting → mediastinal sepsis; Gastrografin study; aggressive surgery. (Boerhaave perforation)

items.lo: ●●○ Benign neoplasm; traumatic perforation; diaphragmatic hernia; caustic ingest
items.text: Caustic ingestion = emergency; NO neutralization, NO induced emesis, NO steroids; airway + endoscopy. Bochdalek (posterolateral) vs Larrey (anterior).

los.statement: ●●● Outline a workup for a patient presenting with various esophageal complaints
●●● Understand the assessment and treatment of various esophageal disorders, based on a case-presentation model
●●○ Understand the various screening techniques for esophageal disorders and malignancies

items.lo: ●●● Outline a workup for a patient presenting with various esophageal complaints
items.text: A patient says food "gets stuck." What two history details most quickly separate a mechanical lesion from a motility disorder, and which single test usually comes first?

items.lo: ●●● Outline a workup for a patient presenting with various esophageal complaints
items.text: Oropharyngeal = trouble initiating the swallow (neck); esophageal = food sticks seconds later (retrosternal). (dysphagia localization)

items.lo: ●●● Outline a workup for a patient presenting with various esophageal complaints
items.text: Globus = painless lump felt irrespective of swallowing, often relieved by it — not true dysphagia. (globus vs dysphagia)

items.topic: THE FIRST FORK — solids vs liquids
items.lo: ●●● Outline a workup for a patient presenting with various esophageal complaints
items.text: Solids-then-liquids = mechanical/obstructing lesion; solids and liquids from onset = motility disorder. (the solid-vs-liquid fork)

items.lo: ●●● Outline a workup for a patient presenting with various esophageal complaints
items.text: Esophageal chest pain can be indistinguishable from cardiac pain, and nitroglycerin relief does not separate them — exclude cardiac first. (esophageal vs cardiac pain)

items.lo: ●●● Outline a workup for a patient presenting with various esophageal complaints
items.text: Suspected proximal stricture → barium swallow before EGD; EGD then diagnoses + excludes malignancy + treats; manometry only if EGD is non-diagnostic. (testing order)

items.lo: ●●● Understand the assessment and treatment of various esophageal disorders, bas
items.text: This is the core of the lecture. As you read each disease, hold one question: what single feature in the vignette — a pattern of dysphagia, a culprit pill, an immune status, a salmon-colored tongue of mucosa — forces the diagnosis and sets the next step?

items.topic: GERD — the root that feeds the rest
items.lo: ●●● Understand the assessment and treatment of various esophageal disorders, bas
items.text: GERD work-up is empiric: lifestyle → PPI trial → EGD only if red flags (dysphagia, weight loss, anemia, bleeding) or failure of therapy. (GERD ladder)

items.topic: Barrett’s esophagus — metaplasia with a number attached
items.lo: ●●● Understand the assessment and treatment of various esophageal disorders, bas
items.text: Barrett’s = salmon mucosa + columnar lining ≥1 cm + goblet-cell intestinal metaplasia on biopsy. All three. It is a cancer-risk marker, not a symptom disease. (Barrett criteria)

items.lo: ●●● Understand the assessment and treatment of various esophageal disorders, bas
items.text: EAC: distal esophagus, arises from Barrett’s, White males, progressive dysphagia + weight loss. Most common EC in the US. (EAC profile)

items.lo: ●●● Understand the assessment and treatment of various esophageal disorders, bas
items.text: SCC: mid-esophagus, tobacco + alcohol (>90% Western cases), most common EC worldwide. EAC vs SCC is decided by risk factors + location, not symptoms. (SCC vs EAC)

items.topic: Esophageal varices — the cross-link to the liver
items.lo: ●●● Understand the assessment and treatment of various esophageal disorders, bas
items.text: Varices are asymptomatic until they bleed; hematemesis + portal hypertension is the link. They point to liver disease — EGD diagnoses and predicts bleeding risk. (varices → liver)

items.topic: Medication-induced (pill) esophagitis — the history makes it
items.lo: ●●● Understand the assessment and treatment of various esophageal disorders, bas
items.text: Pill esophagitis = sudden odynophagia hours-to-days after a culprit pill (bisphosphonate, doxycycline, KCl, NSAID, iron). Diagnosed by history; treat by stopping the pill. (pill esophagitis)

items.topic: Eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE) — the young atopic dysphagic
items.lo: ●●● Understand the assessment and treatment of various esophageal disorders, bas
items.text: EoE = young + atopic + solid dysphagia/impaction + refractory to acid suppression; rings/trachealization, >15 eos/hpf. Treat with diet, steroids/PPI, dilation. (EoE picture)

items.topic: Candida esophagitis — read the immune status
items.lo: ●●● Understand the assessment and treatment of various esophageal disorders, bas
items.text: Candida esophagitis: immunosuppressed host + odynophagia (hallmark) ± thrush. Most commonly C. albicans; treat with fluconazole. (Candida context)

items.lo: ●●○ Understand the various screening techniques for esophageal disorders and mal
items.text: Two patients have heartburn. One gets reassurance and a PPI; the other gets a scope. What separates them — and once Barrett’s is found, what decides how often you look again?

items.topic: The red-flag gate — who earns an EGD
items.lo: ●●○ Understand the various screening techniques for esophageal disorders and mal
items.text: Heartburn is treated empirically; an alarm feature (age ≥60 new onset, dysphagia, weight loss, anemia, bleeding) is what triggers EGD. (red-flag gate)

items.topic: The red-flag gate — who earns an EGD
items.lo: ●●○ Understand the various screening techniques for esophageal disorders and mal
items.text: Barrett’s screening targets a risk-factor stack: GERD ≥5 yr + age ≥50 + male + White + obesity + smoking + family history — not every refluxer. (who to screen)

items.topic: Surveillance — the interval rides on dysplasia
items.lo: ●●○ Understand the various screening techniques for esophageal disorders and mal
items.text: Barrett’s surveillance interval is set by dysplasia grade (3 mo → 5 yr); dysplasia adds endoscopic resection / RFA. Esophagectomy is no longer first-line.

los.statement: ●●● Discuss the differential diagnosis of pain in the RUQ, RLQ, LUQ, LLQ, suprapubic and epigastric area
●○○ Discuss the differential diagnosis of diffuse abdominal pain
●●● Discuss the causes of life-threatening abdominal pain, such as AAA, perforated viscus, ischemic bowel, hemorrhage
●●○ Discuss the causes of non-emergency acute abdomen

items.lo: ●●● Discuss the differential diagnosis of pain in the RUQ, RLQ, LUQ, LLQ, suprap
items.text: A patient points to one quadrant and says the pain stays there. Why does that single fact — that it localizes — make your job so much easier than diffuse pain does?

items.lo: ●●● Discuss the differential diagnosis of pain in the RUQ, RLQ, LUQ, LLQ, suprap
items.text: Pain that localizes → organ in that quadrant → short differential. RUQ liver/gallbladder, epigastric stomach/pancreas, RLQ appendix, LLQ sigmoid, suprapubic bladder. (quadrant → organ)

items.lo: ●●● Discuss the differential diagnosis of pain in the RUQ, RLQ, LUQ, LLQ, suprap
items.text: In gastric cancer the abdominal pain is often a late sign — a reason it is caught late. (gastric cancer pain timing)

items.lo: ●○○ Discuss the differential diagnosis of diffuse abdominal pain
items.text: Diffuse (non-localizing) pain → think generalized peritonitis, early visceral pain, or a metabolic/systemic cause — and screen for the life-threatening four.

items.lo: ●●● Discuss the causes of life-threatening abdominal pain, such as AAA, perforat
items.text: Of every cause of an acute abdomen, four must be known cold because they go straight to surgery. What are they, and what is the single finding that turns each one from “maybe” into “emergency”?

items.lo: ●●● Discuss the causes of life-threatening abdominal pain, such as AAA, perforat
items.text: Life-threatening = surgery now: ruptured AAA, perforated viscus, ischemic bowel, major hemorrhage. Everything else is triaged against these four. (the surgical four)

items.lo: ●●● Discuss the causes of life-threatening abdominal pain, such as AAA, perforat
items.text: Ruptured AAA triad = hypotension + pulsatile mass + back/abdominal pain; most are infrarenal. Surgery, not observation. (AAA triad)

items.topic: PERFORATED VISCUS — read the free air
items.lo: ●●● Discuss the causes of life-threatening abdominal pain, such as AAA, perforat
items.text: Free air under the diaphragm on an upright film = pneumoperitoneum = perforated viscus = emergency. Never read it as benign gas. (free air)

items.lo: ●●● Discuss the causes of life-threatening abdominal pain, such as AAA, perforat
items.text: Mesenteric ischemia = pain out of proportion + increased WBC + acidosis (lactate), classically with AF/embolic risk. Dead bowel = surgery. (mesenteric ischemia triad)

items.lo: ●●○ Discuss the causes of non-emergency acute abdomen
items.text: Some acute abdomens you can watch. What are the classic observe-able causes, and what change at the bedside means you stop observing and operate?

items.lo: ●●○ Discuss the causes of non-emergency acute abdomen
items.text: Observe-able: early appendicitis, uncomplicated cholecystitis/cholelithiasis, PID, pancreatitis. Rule: if pain worsens or peritoneal signs appear → operate. (observe vs operate)

los.statement: ●●● List the signs, symptoms, and management of acute appendicitis
●●○ Formulate a differential diagnosis of acute appendicitis
●●○ Recommend investigations for acute abdomen
●○○ Describe the incidence and management of appendiceal carcinoid

items.lo: ●●● List the signs, symptoms, and management of acute appendicitis
items.text: Appendicitis is the spine of the second half of this topic. What is the classic pain migration, what are the four named signs (and the two the prof said to know cold), and why is treating promptly non-negotiable?

items.lo: ●●● List the signs, symptoms, and management of acute appendicitis
items.text: Appendicitis pain migrates periumbilical → RLQ; untreated, perforation → sepsis → death. That risk is why you treat promptly. (migration + why urgent)

items.topic: WHY THE EXAM IS HARD — location varies
items.lo: ●●● List the signs, symptoms, and management of acute appendicitis
items.text: Appendix location: ~65% retrocecal, 30% pelvic, 5% other — the variability is why the classic signs can be absent and the special maneuvers matter.

items.lo: ●●● List the signs, symptoms, and management of acute appendicitis
items.text: Rovsing press LLQ → pain RLQ; psoas hip extension → pain (retrocecal); obturator hip flexion + internal rotation → pain (pelvic); McBurney RLQ point tenderness. (the four signs)

items.lo: ●●○ Formulate a differential diagnosis of acute appendicitis
items.text: RLQ pain is not always the appendix. How does the differential shift by population — young woman, patient over 60, child — and why are pregnant, elderly, and immunocompromised patients the dangerous ones to diagnose?

items.lo: ●●○ Formulate a differential diagnosis of acute appendicitis
items.text: RLQ differential by population: young woman → ovarian torsion/cyst/ectopic/PID; >60 → cecal carcinoma; child → gastroenteritis/mesenteric lymphadenitis/intussusception. (population differential)

items.lo: ●●○ Formulate a differential diagnosis of acute appendicitis
items.text: Dangerous to diagnose: pregnancy (appendix migrates up; US/MRI), elderly (atypical, ~30% rupture, higher mortality), immunocompromised (blunted signs). Low threshold to image. (high-risk populations)

items.lo: ●●○ Recommend investigations for acute abdomen
items.text: A reproductive-age woman walks in with abdominal pain. What is the first test, full stop — and how does that single result steer which imaging you order next?

items.lo: ●●○ Recommend investigations for acute abdomen
items.text: Reproductive-age abdominal pain: β-hCG is the first test, before imaging — catches ectopic and decides whether CT radiation is safe. (hCG first)

items.lo: ●●○ Recommend investigations for acute abdomen
items.text: Imaging: CT workhorse; US first in children/pregnancy and for gallbladder/gyn; MRI in pregnancy after US; upright film for free air; WBC for inflammation. (imaging selection)

items.lo: ●○○ Describe the incidence and management of appendiceal carcinoid
items.text: Appendiceal carcinoid: usually incidental and small; appendectomy for small lesions, right hemicolectomy for >2 cm or base involvement; carcinoid syndrome uncommon. (carcinoid management)

los.statement: ●○○ Describe the physiological basis of irritable bowel syndrome
●●● Describe irritable bowel syndrome, including Rome IV criteria, alarm symptoms, etiology, and treatment
●●○ Describe the evaluation and management of gluten enteropathy workup for irritable bowel syndrome
●●○ Describe the differential diagnosis of irritable bowel syndrome

items.lo: ●○○ Describe the physiological basis of irritable bowel syndrome
items.text: IBS = functional brain-gut disorder (visceral hypersensitivity + altered motility), no structural lesion — hence a diagnosis of exclusion. Post-infectious subset: anti-vinculin/anti-CdtB molecular mimicry. (functional, no lesion)

items.lo: ●●● Describe irritable bowel syndrome, including Rome IV criteria, alarm symptom
items.text: This is the lecture’s spine and its most board-flagged content. What single sentence in a stem screams IBS, what are the Rome IV criteria behind it, what forces you to stop and work the patient up, and how does subtype steer treatment?

items.lo: ●●● Describe irritable bowel syndrome, including Rome IV criteria, alarm symptom
items.text: A stem saying the pain is relieved by defecation is the IBS board tell — it is the Rome IV “related to defecation” criterion in disguise. (the board tell)

items.lo: ●●● Describe irritable bowel syndrome, including Rome IV criteria, alarm symptom
items.text: Rome IV = recurrent pain ≥1 day/week × 3 months + ≥2 of: related to defecation, change in stool frequency, change in stool form. (Rome IV criteria)

items.topic: SUBTYPE BY BRISTOL STOOL FORM SCALE
items.lo: ●●● Describe irritable bowel syndrome, including Rome IV criteria, alarm symptom
items.text: Subtype by predominant Bristol form: IBS-C hard/lumpy (BSFS 1–2), IBS-D loose/watery (BSFS 6–7), IBS-M mixed — and it sets the drug choice. (BSFS subtyping)

items.topic: DIAGNOSIS OF EXCLUSION — CLEAR THE ALARM FEATURES
items.lo: ●●● Describe irritable bowel syndrome, including Rome IV criteria, alarm symptom
items.text: IBS is a diagnosis of exclusion; alarm features (hematochezia, melena, nocturnal diarrhea, weight loss, anemia, family history) → workup regardless of age. Limited workup clears organic disease in >95% without alarms. (exclusion + alarms)

items.lo: ●●○ Describe the evaluation and management of gluten enteropathy workup for irri
items.text: Celiac disease mimics IBS, so it sits inside the exclusion workup. Which serology do you send first, which confirms it, and what does a normal limited workup actually buy you?

items.lo: ●●○ Describe the evaluation and management of gluten enteropathy workup for irri
items.text: Celiac serology: TTG-IgA first-line, EMA-IgA confirmatory (check total IgA to avoid a false negative). Post-infectious IBS → anti-vinculin/anti-CdtB instead. (celiac serology order)

items.lo: ●●○ Describe the evaluation and management of gluten enteropathy workup for irri
items.text: No alarm features + a limited workup (labs, celiac serology, calprotectin if indicated) excludes organic disease in >95% — the basis of diagnosing IBS by exclusion. (limited workup yield)

items.lo: ●●○ Describe the differential diagnosis of irritable bowel syndrome
items.text: The prof opened the whole lecture with a warning about one confusion. What is it, how do you tell the two apart, and which “associated condition” is a classic distractor that is NOT actually linked to IBS?

items.lo: ●●○ Describe the differential diagnosis of irritable bowel syndrome
items.text: IBS ≠ IBD. IBS = functional, normal labs/endoscopy; IBD = inflammatory, elevated calprotectin + mucosal disease + systemic signs. Read the stem for objective findings. (IBS vs IBD)

items.topic: WHAT IBS IS ASSOCIATED WITH — AND WHAT IT IS NOT
items.lo: ●●○ Describe the differential diagnosis of irritable bowel syndrome
items.text: IBS associates with anxiety/depression/PTSD/fibromyalgia, NOT colorectal cancer. Colon cancer is a differential/alarm consideration — a classic distractor as an “association.” (associations vs distractor)

items.lo: Describe the anatomy of the stomach
items.text: Why does a posterior gastric or duodenal ulcer bleed so much more dramatically than an anterior one?

items.lo: Describe the anatomy of the stomach
items.text: The gastroduodenal artery lies posterior to the stomach/duodenal bulb — a posterior ulcer erodes into it and causes massive hemorrhage (GDA posterior = bleeder)

items.lo: Describe the anatomy of the stomach
items.text: Parietal + chief cells = fundus; G cells (gastrin) = antrum — the map every gastric operation is designed around (cell zonation)

items.lo: Compare and contrast gastric and duodenal ulcers as to clinical presentation and
items.text: Two ulcer patients describe opposite relationships between eating and pain. How do you sort duodenal from gastric, and which one must you biopsy?

items.lo: Compare and contrast gastric and duodenal ulcers as to clinical presentation and
items.text: Duodenal ulcer: pain relieved by food, worse with fasting, wakes the patient at night; weight stable or up early (DU pain pattern)

items.lo: Compare and contrast gastric and duodenal ulcers as to clinical presentation and
items.text: Gastric ulcer: pain with food → the patient avoids eating → weight loss (opposite of duodenal) (GU pain pattern)

items.topic: The bleeding itself carries a clock
items.lo: Compare and contrast gastric and duodenal ulcers as to clinical presentation and
items.text: Bright red hematemesis = fresh bleed; coffee-ground emesis = blood digested by acid (sat longer) (hematemesis clock)

items.topic: Here is the modern reality Rose kept returning to
items.lo: Compare and contrast gastric and duodenal ulcers as to clinical presentation and
items.text: Operate on PUD only for hemorrhage, perforation, obstruction, or intractability — the four complications (surgical indications)

items.lo: Describe common side effects associated with duodenal and gastric ulcer surgery
items.text: A handful of post-gastrectomy syndromes all present with vague abdominal misery. What is the one organizing principle that lets you sort them on a vignette?

items.topic: Early dumping hits within about 15–30 minutes
items.lo: Describe common side effects associated with duodenal and gastric ulcer surgery
items.text: Early dumping = osmotic, ~15–30 min, vasomotor; late dumping = reactive hypoglycemia, hours later (dumping by timing)

items.topic: Afferent-loop obstruction is specific to Billroth II anatomy
items.lo: Describe common side effects associated with duodenal and gastric ulcer surgery
items.text: Afferent-loop (Billroth II): bilious projectile vomiting that relieves pain; alkaline reflux: constant pain, vomiting that does not relieve it (loop vs reflux tell)

items.lo: List the clinical and laboratory features that differentiate Zollinger-Ellison s
items.text: What single provocative test separates a gastrinoma from ordinary duodenal ulcer disease, and which way does the result move?

items.lo: List the clinical and laboratory features that differentiate Zollinger-Ellison s
items.text: Secretin normally suppresses gastrin; in ZES it paradoxically raises gastrin — the diagnostic test (secretin paradox)

items.lo: List the clinical and laboratory features that differentiate Zollinger-Ellison s
items.text: ZES basal acid output ~10× normal with little further rise on stimulation (parietal mass already maximal) (BAO 10x)

items.lo: Identify pre-malignant conditions and clinical features in patients with gastric
items.text: A patient with longstanding pernicious anemia develops vague epigastric symptoms. Why does this combination demand urgent attention, and what are the four named metastases you are screening for?

items.topic: Three conditions are flagged as premalignant
items.lo: Identify pre-malignant conditions and clinical features in patients with gastric
items.text: Pernicious anemia / atrophic gastritis is premalignant — ~40% progress to gastric carcinoma (PA premalignant 40%)

items.lo: Identify pre-malignant conditions and clinical features in patients with gastric
items.text: Four named gastric-cancer mets: Virchow (L supraclavicular), Krukenberg (ovary), Blumer shelf (rectouterine), Sister Mary Joseph (umbilicus) (eponymous mets)

items.lo: Describe common types of neoplasms of the stomach
items.text: Endoscopy shows a smooth submucosal gastric mass with a central crater. What is it, and how is it treated now versus in the past?

items.lo: Describe common types of neoplasms of the stomach
items.text: Leiomyoma: smooth submucosal mass with central ulceration at endoscopy; resect (leiomyoma morphology)

items.lo: List the general principles of curative and palliative surgical procedures for p
items.text: Two gastric-cancer patients reach the OR — one with localized disease, one whose tumor has already perforated and seeded the peritoneum. How does the surgical goal differ?

items.lo: List the general principles of curative and palliative surgical procedures for p
items.text: Curative extended total gastrectomy = stomach + spleen, distal pancreas, parapyloric nodes, omentum (gastrectomy field)

items.lo: Describe the clinical presentation and treatment of pyloric stenosis
items.text: A newborn vomits forcefully and a thick "olive" is palpable. Who classically gets this, and what fixes it?

items.lo: Describe the clinical presentation and treatment of pyloric stenosis
items.text: Pyloric stenosis: first-born male, projectile vomiting, palpable olive → pyloromyotomy after rehydration (pyloric stenosis triad + fix)

items.lo: Describe the indications and complications associated with bariatric surgery
items.text: Who qualifies for bariatric surgery, which operation is now the gold standard, and what nutritional price does the bypass exact?

items.lo: Describe the indications and complications associated with bariatric surgery
items.text: Bariatric candidacy: BMI ≥40 (or ≥100 lb over ideal); loosened for the type-2 diabetic (BMI gate)

items.lo: Describe the indications and complications associated with bariatric surgery
items.text: Roux-en-Y bypasses the duodenum → iron, calcium, B12 malabsorption → anemia + metabolic bone disease; lifelong supplements (RYGB deficiencies)

items.topic: Two more bypass consequences worth carrying
items.lo: Describe the indications and complications associated with bariatric surgery
items.text: Rapid post-bariatric weight loss → ↓CCK → gallbladder stasis → gallstones (cholelithiasis) (bariatric gallstones)

items.lo: Describe the congenital disorders of the small intestine, such as atresia, steno
items.text: A newborn vomits bile in the first day of life. What congenital lesions are on your list, and which one carries the "rule of 2s"?

items.lo: Describe the congenital disorders of the small intestine, such as atresia, steno
items.text: Duodenal atresia/stenosis — associated with Down syndrome; bilious vomiting in the first 24–48 h (Down + bilious vomiting)

items.lo: Describe the congenital disorders of the small intestine, such as atresia, steno
items.text: Meckel rule of 2s: 2% of population, 2 ft of ileocecal valve, 2 in long, age 2, 2 ectopic mucosa types (gastric + pancreatic) (rule of 2s)

items.lo: Describe the congenital disorders of the small intestine, such as atresia, steno
items.text: Meconium ileus — thick meconium plugs the ileum; classic for cystic fibrosis (no meconium passed in first 12–24 h) (meconium ileus = CF)

items.lo: Describe the main types and mechanisms of mechanical obstruction of the small in
items.text: What are the four mechanical causes of small-bowel obstruction, and what single word tells you the blood supply is now in danger?

items.lo: Describe the main types and mechanisms of mechanical obstruction of the small in
items.text: Strangulation = obstruction has compromised the blood supply → ischemia → gangrene (the danger word in SBO) (strangulation = danger word)

items.lo: Describe the main types and mechanisms of mechanical obstruction of the small in
items.text: Four SBO mechanisms: hernia, adhesions, volvulus, intussusception (adhesions = most common in adults) (four SBO causes)

items.lo: Describe the concept of ischemic bowel disease, encompassing the acute and chron
items.text: Machini drilled one pathology sequence repeatedly. What is it, and how does it sort acute arterial occlusion from chronic mesenteric ischemia?

items.lo: Describe the concept of ischemic bowel disease, encompassing the acute and chron
items.text: Pathology sequence: inflammation → ischemia (recoverable) → infarction (necrosis, dead); cells don’t die day one (inflammation→ischemia→infarction)

items.lo: Describe the concept of ischemic bowel disease, encompassing the acute and chron
items.text: Acute SMA occlusion (embolus/thrombus) → edematous purple-red bowel + pneumatosis + absent peristalsis (acute occlusion signs)

items.lo: Describe the concept of ischemic bowel disease, encompassing the acute and chron
items.text: Abdominal angina = chronic mesenteric ischemia: postprandial pain → food fear → weight loss (abdominal angina triad)

items.lo: Describe the normal intestinal absorption, the luminal and intestinal malabsorpt
items.text: Once you have diagnosed malabsorption, Machini said the next job is to localize it. Localize it to what?

items.lo: Describe the normal intestinal absorption, the luminal and intestinal malabsorpt
items.text: Three absorptive phases: intraluminal (luminal digestion) → intestinal (brush border) → transepithelial (into blood) (three phases)

items.lo: Describe the normal intestinal absorption, the luminal and intestinal malabsorpt
items.text: General malabsorption (all nutrients) = celiac; isolated (one nutrient) = B12 in terminal-ileal disease → megaloblastic anemia (general vs isolated)

items.lo: Describe the etiology, pathogenesis, morphological, and clinical features of lac
items.text: Three classic small-bowel malabsorptive diseases share a final picture of mucosal damage. What is the distinct mechanism and key buzzword for each?

items.lo: Describe the etiology, pathogenesis, morphological, and clinical features of lac
items.text: Lactase deficiency: osmotic diarrhea/bloating/flatus after milk; lactose tolerance test is diagnostic (lactase deficiency)

items.lo: Describe the etiology, pathogenesis, morphological, and clinical features of lac
items.text: Celiac: gluten on HLA-DQ2/DQ8 → CD4/CD8 attack → villous atrophy + mucosal mosaic on duodenal biopsy (celiac mechanism)

items.lo: Describe the etiology, pathogenesis, morphological, and clinical features of lac
items.text: Whipple: Tropheryma whipplei (gram-positive actinomycete) fills PAS-positive macrophages → blocked lymphatic fat transport (Whipple = gram-positive)

items.lo: Describe the main types of benign, malignant, and neuroendocrine tumors of the s
items.text: The small bowel hosts several tumor families. Which polyposis did Machini call number-one important, and what buzzword pins the carcinoid?

items.lo: Describe the main types of benign, malignant, and neuroendocrine tumors of the s
items.text: FAP (APC, autosomal dominant) — near-100% cancer by age 30; Machini’s number-one-important polyposis (FAP = APC, 100% by 30)

items.lo: Describe the main types of benign, malignant, and neuroendocrine tumors of the s
items.text: GIST: cells of Cajal, c-KIT mutation; <2 cm low-risk, >5 cm aggressive (GIST = Cajal + c-KIT)

items.lo: Describe the main types of benign, malignant, and neuroendocrine tumors of the s
items.text: Carcinoid: salt-and-pepper chromatin, chromogranin A positive, appendix/rectum (>5% duodenal) (carcinoid buzzwords)

Describe the signs and symptoms, differential diagnosis, and therapy of Crohn’s disease
Discuss the history, etiology, signs and symptoms, differential diagnosis, and therapy of ulcerative
Compare and contrast Crohn’s disease and ulcerative colitis

items.lo: List the conditions associated with inflammatory bowel diseases
items.text: Lamet called IBD a "diagnosis of exclusion." What has to be ruled out first, and what is the one-sentence explanation for why IBD happens at all?

items.lo: List the conditions associated with inflammatory bowel diseases
items.text: IBD is a diagnosis of exclusion — rule out infectious, ischemic, radiation, diverticular, and drug/toxin colitis first (exclusion differential)

items.lo: List the conditions associated with inflammatory bowel diseases
items.text: IBD = failure to distinguish foreign from self + failure to down-regulate the mucosal immune response (genetic + immunologic) (core mechanism)

items.lo: Discuss the etiology, epidemiology, and pathology of Crohn’s disease
items.text: What four pathology features define Crohn’s disease, and which one is pathognomonic on biopsy?

items.lo: Discuss the etiology, epidemiology, and pathology of Crohn’s disease
items.text: Crohn’s is transmural (full-thickness) → fistulas and strictures; UC is mucosa-only and makes neither (transmural = Crohn’s)

items.topic: colon, then another inflamed transverse segment
items.lo: Discuss the etiology, epidemiology, and pathology of Crohn’s disease
items.text: Crohn’s: mouth to anus, commonest at terminal ileum, discontinuous skip lesions, cobblestone mucosa (distribution + skip lesions)

items.lo: Discuss the etiology, epidemiology, and pathology of Crohn’s disease
items.text: Non-caseating granuloma on biopsy = pathognomonic for Crohn’s (vs crypt abscess in UC) (granuloma = Crohn’s)

items.lo: Describe the signs and symptoms, differential diagnosis, and therapy of Crohn’s
items.text: Lamet built the therapy of Crohn’s as a ladder. What is the rough order, and which biologic carries the PML risk that took it out of favor?

items.lo: Describe the signs and symptoms, differential diagnosis, and therapy of Crohn’s
items.text: Therapy goals: induce + maintain remission, scaled to extent and severity; terminal-ileal Crohn’s → B12 deficiency (goals + B12)

items.lo: Describe the signs and symptoms, differential diagnosis, and therapy of Crohn’s
items.text: Sulfasalazine: a diazo bond lets colonic bacteria release active 5-ASA in the colon (plain 5-ASA is absorbed too early) (5-ASA delivery)

items.lo: Describe the signs and symptoms, differential diagnosis, and therapy of Crohn’s
items.text: Budesonide = locally-acting steroid, minimal systemic absorption; steroids induce but don’t maintain (budesonide niche)

items.lo: Describe the signs and symptoms, differential diagnosis, and therapy of Crohn’s
items.text: Anti-TNF by humanness: infliximab chimeric (75%) → certolizumab (95%) → adalimumab fully human (100%); more human = less immunogenic

items.lo: Describe the signs and symptoms, differential diagnosis, and therapy of Crohn’s
items.text: Natalizumab → PML risk; vedolizumab is gut-selective with no PML; surgery in Crohn’s is NOT curative (PML + surgery caveat)

items.lo: Discuss the history, etiology, signs and symptoms, differential diagnosis, and t
items.text: Lamet gave ulcerative colitis three defining adjectives in his first sentence about it. What are they, and what biopsy finding is its histologic signature?

items.lo: Discuss the history, etiology, signs and symptoms, differential diagnosis, and t
items.text: UC = starts in rectum + contiguous + mucosa-only (no skip lesions, no transmural spread) (UC three adjectives)

items.lo: Discuss the history, etiology, signs and symptoms, differential diagnosis, and t
items.text: UC extent: proctitis (rectum) → left-sided → pancolitis; endoscopy shows loss of vascular pattern + granularity (UC extent + endoscopy)

items.lo: Discuss the history, etiology, signs and symptoms, differential diagnosis, and t
items.text: UC biopsy = crypt abscess; chronic colon = lead-pipe (lost haustra); cancer risk ~10% per 10 yr (crypt abscess + lead-pipe)

items.lo: Discuss the history, etiology, signs and symptoms, differential diagnosis, and t
items.text: UC therapy by severity: topical 5-ASA/steroid for proctitis → IV steroids + anti-TNF for severe; colectomy is curative in UC (UC therapy + curative surgery)

items.lo: Compare and contrast Crohn’s disease and ulcerative colitis
items.text: If a vignette hands you one feature — a granuloma, a fistula, a skip lesion, a lead-pipe colon — how do you instantly assign it to UC or Crohn’s?

items.lo: Compare and contrast Crohn’s disease and ulcerative colitis
items.text: Depth axis: fistula/stricture/abscess → Crohn’s (transmural); toxic megacolon → UC (mucosal but extensive) (depth → complications)

items.lo: Compare and contrast Crohn’s disease and ulcerative colitis
items.text: Buzzword sort — UC: crypt abscess, lead-pipe, continuous, curative colectomy. Crohn’s: granuloma, cobblestone, skip lesions, non-curative surgery

items.lo: Compare and contrast Crohn’s disease and ulcerative colitis
items.text: Smoking is protective in UC, harmful in Crohn’s (the classic paradox) (smoking paradox)

items.lo: Discuss the pathology of diaphragmatic hernia, and congenital and acquired pylor
items.text: Chakraborty called pyloric stenosis "very high yield." What is its classic infant presentation, and what single electrolyte derangement does the exam key off?

items.lo: Discuss the pathology of diaphragmatic hernia, and congenital and acquired pylor
items.text: Omphalocele = midline, sac-covered, syndrome-associated; gastroschisis = para-umbilical, no sac (sac vs no sac)

items.lo: Discuss the pathology of diaphragmatic hernia, and congenital and acquired pylor
items.text: Congenital diaphragmatic hernia (usually left) → pulmonary hypoplasia = most common cause of death; Bochdalek (posterolateral) vs Morgagni (anterior)

items.lo: Discuss the pathology of diaphragmatic hernia, and congenital and acquired pylor
items.text: Hypertrophic pyloric stenosis: 3–5× males, projectile non-bilious vomiting at 3–6 wk, palpable olive, left-to-right peristalsis (pyloric stenosis picture)

items.lo: Discuss the pathology of diaphragmatic hernia, and congenital and acquired pylor
items.text: Pyloric stenosis → hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis; ultrasound = initial test; Ramstedt pyloromyotomy = treatment (alkalosis + Ramstedt)

items.lo: Discuss the pathogenesis and pathological features of acute gastritis and stress
items.text: Two named stress ulcers show up on every exam. Which follows burns, which follows head injury, and what is the mechanism of each?

items.lo: Discuss the pathogenesis and pathological features of acute gastritis and stress
items.text: Gastropathy = inflammatory cells absent, epithelium intact (NSAIDs, alcohol, bile); gastritis = neutrophils infiltrating + erosion (gastropathy vs gastritis)

items.lo: Discuss the pathogenesis and pathological features of acute gastritis and stress
items.text: Acute gastritis = damaging forces (acid, pepsin) > defensive forces (mucus, bicarbonate, blood flow, prostaglandins); NSAIDs strip prostaglandins

items.lo: Discuss the pathogenesis and pathological features of acute gastritis and stress
items.text: Curling = burns/shock → mucosal ischemia; Cushing = raised ICP → vagal → acid hypersecretion (Curling vs Cushing)

items.lo: List the types, and discuss the pathogenesis, morphology, and complications of c
items.text: Chronic gastritis splits into a few types Chakraborty wants you to distinguish. How do H. pylori and autoimmune gastritis differ in location, acid level, and complication?

items.lo: List the types, and discuss the pathogenesis, morphology, and complications of c
items.text: H. pylori: antral-predominant, CagA, Warthin-Starry stain, antrum biopsy site, triple therapy (PPI + clarithromycin + amoxicillin) (H. pylori workup)

items.lo: List the types, and discuss the pathogenesis, morphology, and complications of c
items.text: Chronic H. pylori → MALT lymphoma and adenocarcinoma (a class 1 carcinogen) (H. pylori complications)

items.lo: List the types, and discuss the pathogenesis, morphology, and complications of c
items.text: Autoimmune gastritis: anti-parietal + anti-intrinsic-factor → hypochlorhydria + B12 deficiency/pernicious anemia; fundus/corpus, spares antrum

items.lo: List the types, and discuss the pathogenesis, morphology, and complications of c
items.text: Ménétrier: foveolar hyperplasia (corkscrew glands), hypochlorhydria, protein-losing gastroenteropathy (not Ménière ear disease) (Ménétrier)

items.lo: Discuss the sites, incidence, etiological and pathogenetic factors, morphology,
items.text: A duodenal ulcer and a gastric ulcer differ in how meals affect pain and which artery they threaten. What are the two distinctions Chakraborty flagged as "very important"?

items.lo: Discuss the sites, incidence, etiological and pathogenetic factors, morphology,
items.text: Duodenal ulcer ~90% (anterior, near pylorus, >95% H. pylori); gastric ulcer on lesser curvature; benign = punched-out, malignant = heaped (sites + punched-out)

items.topic: whose pain vanishes the moment he eats is a duodenal ulcer
items.lo: Discuss the sites, incidence, etiological and pathogenetic factors, morphology,
items.text: Duodenal ulcer pain improves with meals; gastric ulcer pain worsens with meals (pain-meal rule)

items.lo: Discuss the sites, incidence, etiological and pathogenetic factors, morphology,
items.text: Posterior duodenal ulcer → gastroduodenal artery hemorrhage (emergency); gastric/lesser curvature → left gastric artery; kissing ulcers → outlet obstruction (artery complications)

items.lo: List the types and discuss the pathology of benign and malignant tumors of the s
items.text: Gastric adenocarcinoma splits into two Lauren types. How do the intestinal and diffuse types differ in genetics, morphology, and the cell Chakraborty made you memorize?

items.lo: List the types and discuss the pathology of benign and malignant tumors of the s
items.text: Gastric polyps: hyperplastic/inflammatory ~90% (benign); fundal (APC); adenomatous ~10% = premalignant (risk rises >2 cm) (gastric polyps)

items.topic: Gastric adenocarcinoma is driven by H
items.lo: List the types and discuss the pathology of benign and malignant tumors of the s
items.text: Lauren: intestinal (~90%, APC, metaplasia, distal, glands) vs diffuse (~10%, CDH1/E-cadherin loss, signet-ring, linitis plastica) (Lauren classification)

items.topic: bilateral ovarian metastasis from signet-ring cells
items.lo: List the types and discuss the pathology of benign and malignant tumors of the s
items.text: Gastric carcinoma mets: Virchow (L supraclavicular), Sister Mary Joseph (periumbilical), Krukenberg (ovaries), Trousseau (migratory thrombophlebitis)

items.text: MALToma: t(11;18)/t(14;18), CD19/20, → DLBCL. GIST: cells of Cajal, KIT/PDGFRA, markers CD117 + DOG1 (MALToma + GIST)

items.lo: Identify the clinical characteristics and diagnosis of parasite infections of th
items.text: This LO is a buzzword-matching exercise. For the high-yield GI parasites, what is the one detail — a stool finding, a deficiency, a complication — that lets you name each on sight?

items.lo: Identify the clinical characteristics and diagnosis of parasite infections of th
items.text: Entamoeba: bloody dysentery + flask-shaped ulcer + anchovy-paste liver abscess → metronidazole (Entamoeba tells)

items.lo: Identify the clinical characteristics and diagnosis of parasite infections of th
items.text: Acid-fast oocysts + watery diarrhea = Cryptosporidium (HIV) / Cyclospora (acid-fast protozoa)

items.lo: Identify the clinical characteristics and diagnosis of parasite infections of th
items.text: Diphyllobothrium → B12 deficiency; Echinococcus → hydatid cyst (don’t aspirate); Clonorchis → cholangiocarcinoma (tapeworm/fluke tells)

items.lo: Identify the clinical characteristics and diagnosis of parasite infections of th
items.text: Taenia solium eggs → cysticercosis (neurocysticercosis) (cysticercosis)

items.topic: syndrome) and in heavy burden causes bowel obstruction
items.lo: Identify the clinical characteristics and diagnosis of parasite infections of th
items.text: Nematode tells: whipworm → rectal prolapse; pinworm → perianal itch + tape test; hookworm → iron-deficiency anemia; Ascaris → Löffler + obstruction

items.lo: Outline the principles of management of parasite infections of the gastrointesti
items.text: Management of GI parasites collapses into a few drug families. Which drug covers the protozoa, which covers most worms, and which is the fluke drug?

items.lo: Outline the principles of management of parasite infections of the gastrointesti
items.text: Metronidazole for Entamoeba/Giardia; TMP-SMX for Cyclospora/Cystoisospora (protozoa drugs)

items.lo: Outline the principles of management of parasite infections of the gastrointesti
items.text: Most nematodes → albendazole/mebendazole (pyrantel for pinworm); Strongyloides → ivermectin (nematode drugs)

items.lo: Outline the principles of management of parasite infections of the gastrointesti
items.text: Praziquantel for tapeworms + Schistosoma + flukes; Echinococcus → albendazole (not praziquantel) (flatworm drugs)

items.lo: Identify the clinical characteristics and diagnosis of fungal infections of the
items.text: Blavo’s fungal section is short — two organisms. What is the host setting and the identifying clue for each?

items.topic: CANDIDA ALBICANS is the commonest GI fungal infection
items.lo: Identify the clinical characteristics and diagnosis of fungal infections of the
items.text: Candida albicans = commonest GI fungus; thrush + esophagitis in immunocompromised/HIV or post-antibiotics; pseudohyphae (Candida tells)

items.topic: CANDIDA ALBICANS is the commonest GI fungal infection
items.lo: Identify the clinical characteristics and diagnosis of fungal infections of the
items.text: Histoplasma = dimorphic, bird/bat droppings, disseminated in immunocompromised; intracellular yeast in macrophages (Histoplasma tells)

items.lo: Outline the principles of management of fungal infections of the gastrointestina
items.text: The two GI fungi map to two antifungal strategies. What is first-line for each, and when do you escalate?

items.lo: Outline the principles of management of fungal infections of the gastrointestina
items.text: Candida: fluconazole first-line (nystatin for mild oral) → echinocandin/amphotericin B for severe/systemic (Candida treatment)

items.lo: Outline the principles of management of fungal infections of the gastrointestina
items.text: Histoplasma: itraconazole (mild-moderate) → amphotericin B first for severe/disseminated, then step down (Histoplasma treatment)

items.lo: ●●● Surgical approach to benign versus malignant lesions of the intestines and r
items.text: Two intestinal lesions on one exam: an adult intussusception and a biopsy-proven small bowel lymphoma. What do you do with each, and why do they break the usual "obstruction → relieve it" rule?

items.text: A patient with exocrine pancreatic insufficiency and a separate patient with lactase non-persistence both have steatorrhea — are these maldigestion or malabsorption, and what is the difference?

items.text: A 30-year-old with chronic diarrhea, iron-deficiency anemia, and an itchy vesicular rash on the elbows has a positive tTG-IgA. What genetic association underlies the disease, and what single test confirms it?
