los.statement: ●○○ Describe the anatomy and function of the large intestine
●○○ List the features of angiodysplasia
●●● Describe the different types of tumors of the colon, rectum, and anal canal
●●● Describe the TNM classification of carcinoma of the colon and rectum
●●○ Describe appendicitis and appendiceal neoplasms
●●○ Describe peritonitis and neoplasms of the peritoneum

items.lo: ●○○ Describe the anatomy and function of the large intestine
items.text: What three histologic features let you call a biopsy “colon” rather than “small intestine”?

items.lo: ●○○ Describe the anatomy and function of the large intestine
items.text: Large intestine ≈ 1.5 m (5 ft), ileum→anus; absorbs water + electrolytes, forms feces (anatomy recall)

items.lo: ●○○ Describe the anatomy and function of the large intestine
items.text: Colonic mucosa = no villi, straight crypts, colonocytes + goblet cells (histology buzzwords)

items.lo: ●○○ List the features of angiodysplasia
items.text: Why does angiodysplasia preferentially affect the cecum rather than the sigmoid?

items.lo: ●○○ List the features of angiodysplasia
items.text: Angiodysplasia = vascular ectasia of the cecum/right colon; ~20–25% of major lower-GI bleeds after the 6th decade (location + epidemiology)

items.lo: ●●● Describe the different types of tumors of the colon, rectum, and anal canal
items.text: A large sessile rectal polyp with finger-like projections is biopsied — which histology is it, and why does that matter?

items.lo: ●●● Describe the different types of tumors of the colon, rectum, and anal canal
items.text: Adenoma risk climbs tubular → tubulovillous → villous; highest when >2 cm, sessile, villous (malignant-potential gradient)

items.topic: POLYPOSIS GENES — recognize them in the options
items.lo: ●●● Describe the different types of tumors of the colon, rectum, and anal canal
items.text: Peutz-Jeghers STK11; juvenile polyposis SMAD4/BMPR1A; Cowden + BRR PTEN; FAP APC (syndrome→gene recognition)

items.lo: ●●● Describe the different types of tumors of the colon, rectum, and anal canal
items.text: Sequence: APC (first hit, Knudson) → KRAS → 18q21/SMAD + TP53/↑COX; aspirin impedes (gene order)

items.topic: FAP vs LYNCH — don’t swap the genes
items.lo: ●●● Describe the different types of tumors of the colon, rectum, and anal canal
items.text: FAP = APC (5q21, carpet of polyps); Lynch = MSH2/MLH1 MMR, MSI, early-onset right-sided (syndrome discrimination)

items.topic: LEFT vs RIGHT CRC — two different pictures
items.lo: ●●● Describe the different types of tumors of the colon, rectum, and anal canal
items.text: Left CRC = apple-core, ↓caliber, blood-streaked stool; right CRC = bulky lesion, iron-deficiency anemia (left vs right presentation)

items.topic: LEFT vs RIGHT CRC — two different pictures
items.lo: ●●● Describe the different types of tumors of the colon, rectum, and anal canal
items.text: Strep bovis endocarditis → work up for colon cancer (classic association)

items.lo: ●●● Describe the different types of tumors of the colon, rectum, and anal canal
items.text: Anal-canal cancer = squamous cell carcinoma, strong HPV 16/18 association (anal SCC buzzword)

items.lo: ●●● Describe the TNM classification of carcinoma of the colon and rectum
items.text: A colon adenocarcinoma is staged T2 N0 M0 — what is the next step, and why?

items.lo: ●●● Describe the TNM classification of carcinoma of the colon and rectum
items.text: T2 N0 M0 = no surgery; T3+ → resection then chemoradiation (surgery threshold)

items.lo: ●●● Describe the TNM classification of carcinoma of the colon and rectum
items.text: CEA = monitoring/recurrence, not screening; rectal cancer operation = TME (CEA + TME)

items.lo: ●●○ Describe appendicitis and appendiceal neoplasms
items.text: How does a mucinous neoplasm of the appendix produce diffuse gelatinous ascites?

items.lo: ●●○ Describe appendicitis and appendiceal neoplasms
items.text: Appendicitis pain migrates periumbilical → RLQ/McBurney; dx needs neutrophils in the muscularis propria (presentation + histology)

items.lo: ●●○ Describe peritonitis and neoplasms of the peritoneum
items.text: In a cirrhotic with ascites, fever, and neutrophilic fluid without a surgical source — what is the diagnosis?

items.lo: ●●○ Describe peritonitis and neoplasms of the peritoneum
items.text: SBP = neutrophilic ascitic fluid in a cirrhotic with no surgical source; chemical peritonitis = bile/enzymes/foreign material (peritonitis types)

los.statement: ●●○ Define constipation
●○○ Discuss the epidemiology of constipation
●●● Describe the different types of constipation
●●○ Discuss the most common drugs causing constipation
●●● Discuss the evaluation of constipation
●●○ Discuss the non-pharmacologic management of constipation

items.lo: ●●○ Define constipation
items.text: A patient strains and passes hard pellets more than a quarter of the time, <3 times a week, for four months — do they meet Rome IV, and what extra criterion must you check?

items.lo: ●●○ Define constipation
items.text: Rome IV functional constipation = ≥2 of 6 for ≥3 mo, each in >25% of defecations; <3 spontaneous BM/wk; insufficient for IBS (definition recall)

items.lo: ●●○ Define constipation
items.text: Bristol type 1–2 (hard lumps / lumpy sausage) = constipation; 6–7 = loose (Bristol direction)

items.lo: ●○○ Discuss the epidemiology of constipation
items.text: Constipation prevalence ~15% of adults; higher in women, non-Caucasians, and age ≥65 (epidemiology recall)

items.lo: ●●● Describe the different types of constipation
items.text: Two patients both have hard, infrequent stool; one has a normal colon-transit study but cannot expel a rectal balloon. Which type is that, and how do you confirm it?

items.lo: ●●● Describe the different types of constipation
items.text: Slow-transit = blunted colonic response to meals/bisacodyl/cholinergics (enteric-plexus dysfunction) (slow-transit physiology)

items.lo: ●●● Describe the different types of constipation
items.text: Dyssynergic = failed abdominal/pelvic-floor coordination (paradoxical puborectalis); dx by manometry + balloon expulsion (dyssynergia definition)

items.lo: ●●● Describe the different types of constipation
items.text: IBS-C vs CIC: the discriminator is abdominal pain related to defecation/stool change (shared symptoms otherwise) (IBS-C discriminator)

items.lo: ●●○ Discuss the most common drugs causing constipation
items.text: Why does taking the medication history change the management of a newly constipated patient?

items.lo: ●●○ Discuss the most common drugs causing constipation
items.text: Constipating drugs: opioids, anticholinergics, TCAs, antihistamines, CCBs, iron (drug-class recall)

items.lo: ●●○ Discuss the most common drugs causing constipation
items.text: OIC = enteric mu-receptor effect: ↓motility/secretion + ↑sphincter tone; has its own Rome IV criteria (OIC mechanism)

items.lo: ●●● Discuss the evaluation of constipation
items.text: A 50-year-old reports new constipation with iron-deficiency anemia — what is the next step in the algorithm, and why?

items.lo: ●●● Discuss the evaluation of constipation
items.text: Run the work-up in order: history (+meds) → DRE → alarm screen → type-specific tests (evaluation sequence)

items.lo: ●●● Discuss the evaluation of constipation
items.text: DRE clue: paradoxical puborectalis contraction on bear-down → dyssynergic defecation (DRE finding)

items.lo: ●●● Discuss the evaluation of constipation
items.text: Alarm features (age ≥45, hematochezia, IDA, weight loss ≥10 lb, obstruction, FHx CRC/IBD) → colonoscopy (alarm screen)

items.lo: ●●● Discuss the evaluation of constipation
items.text: Confirm the type: colon-transit → slow-transit; manometry + balloon expulsion → dyssynergia; then split IBS-C vs CIC (type-specific tests)

items.lo: ●●○ Discuss the non-pharmacologic management of constipation
items.text: A patient with dyssynergic defecation has failed fiber and is asking about surgery — what do you recommend first, and why?

items.lo: ●●○ Discuss the non-pharmacologic management of constipation
items.text: First-line non-pharm = fiber + fluids + behavior (use the morning gastrocolic reflex) (first-line base)

items.lo: ●●○ Discuss the non-pharmacologic management of constipation
items.text: Biofeedback = first-line for refractory/dyssynergic constipation; must precede colectomy (first-line refractory)

items.lo: ●●○ Discuss the non-pharmacologic management of constipation
items.text: Subtotal colectomy = last-resort, slow-transit only; avoid when pain/bloating predominate (likely IBS-C) (surgery indication)

los.statement: ●●● Describe indirect inguinal, direct inguinal hernia and femoral hernias
●●○ List the factors that predispose to the development of inguinal hernias
●●● Discuss the relative frequency of indirect, direct, and femoral hernias by age and sex
●●● Define incarcerated inguinal hernia, strangulated hernia, and sliding hernia
●●● Define incarcerated femoral hernia, and Richter hernia
●●○ Outline the principles of management of patients with groin hernias
●●○ Describe the development of an umbilical hernia
●●○ Discuss the appropriate use of prosthetic materials in hernia repair

items.lo: ●●● Describe indirect inguinal, direct inguinal hernia and femoral hernias
items.text: A young man has a groin bulge that descends into the scrotum and the impulse hits your fingertip — which hernia is it, and where does its sac sit relative to the inferior epigastric vessels?

items.lo: ●●● Describe indirect inguinal, direct inguinal hernia and femoral hernias
items.text: Hesselbach’s triangle = inferior epigastric vessels (lateral) + rectus sheath (medial) + inguinal ligament (inferior); a direct hernia goes through it (board-flagged landmark)

items.lo: ●●● Describe indirect inguinal, direct inguinal hernia and femoral hernias
items.text: Indirect = lateral to inferior epigastric vessels, internal ring, congenital, scrotal-capable, fingertip impulse; direct = medial, posterior wall, acquired, pulp impulse (indirect vs direct)

items.lo: ●●● Describe indirect inguinal, direct inguinal hernia and femoral hernias
items.text: Femoral hernia = through the femoral canal, below the inguinal ligament, medial to the femoral vein; narrow neck → high strangulation risk (femoral route)

items.lo: ●●○ List the factors that predispose to the development of inguinal hernias
items.text: Why does a patent processus vaginalis predispose specifically to an indirect (rather than direct) hernia?

items.lo: ●●○ List the factors that predispose to the development of inguinal hernias
items.text: Predisposing = chronic ↑intra-abdominal pressure (cough/COPD, constipation, prostatism, ascites) + patent processus vaginalis + connective-tissue weakness + age (risk factors)

items.lo: ●●● Discuss the relative frequency of indirect, direct, and femoral hernias by a
items.text: A woman presents with a groin hernia — what is the single most likely type, and what is the relative-frequency fact that the exam wants you to NOT over-apply?

items.lo: ●●● Discuss the relative frequency of indirect, direct, and femoral hernias by a
items.text: Indirect inguinal = most common hernia in both sexes; femoral only relatively more common in women; pediatric hernias favor the right (frequency + female-femoral trap)

items.lo: ●●● Define incarcerated inguinal hernia, strangulated hernia, and sliding hernia
items.text: A groin hernia is irreducible but non-tender with well-perfused contents — which stage is this, and what one-sentence rule keeps it distinct from the next stage?

items.lo: ●●● Define incarcerated inguinal hernia, strangulated hernia, and sliding hernia
items.text: Incarcerated = irreducible, perfusion intact; strangulated = ischemic. Rule: incarceration without strangulation YES, strangulation without incarceration NO

items.lo: ●●● Define incarcerated inguinal hernia, strangulated hernia, and sliding hernia
items.text: Sliding hernia = a retroperitoneal viscus (bladder, sigmoid, cecum, ovary) forms part of the sac wall (sliding definition)

items.lo: ●●● Define incarcerated femoral hernia, and Richter hernia
items.text: Why can a Richter hernia strangulate and necrose bowel without producing a bowel obstruction?

items.topic: RICHTER HERNIA — they love it on the boards
items.lo: ●●● Define incarcerated femoral hernia, and Richter hernia
items.text: Femoral = highest incarceration/strangulation risk (narrow neck); Richter = partial-circumference trap that strangulates with a patent lumen (no obstruction) (femoral + Richter danger)

items.lo: ●●● Define incarcerated femoral hernia, and Richter hernia
items.text: Obturator = Howship-Romberg (thin elderly woman + SBO); Littre = contains Meckel’s; Spigelian = linea semilunaris; pantaloon = direct + indirect together (named-hernia catalog)

items.lo: ●●○ Outline the principles of management of patients with groin hernias
items.text: Why is forceful reduction of a tender, irreducible groin hernia dangerous, and what should you do instead?

items.lo: ●●○ Outline the principles of management of patients with groin hernias
items.text: Don’t force-reduce a tender/incarcerated hernia (risk: reducing dead bowel) → prompt surgical evaluation; a truss only temporizes (safety rule)

items.lo: ●●○ Describe the development of an umbilical hernia
items.text: How does the management of a small reducible umbilical hernia differ between a 1-year-old and an adult, and why?

items.lo: ●●○ Describe the development of an umbilical hernia
items.text: Pediatric umbilical hernia → resolves spontaneously, ignore until preschool unless incarcerated; adult umbilical = acquired (pediatric vs adult)

items.lo: ●●○ Describe the development of an umbilical hernia
items.text: Omphalocele = membrane-covered, midline; gastroschisis = no sac, lateral to umbilicus (exposed bowel, more severe) (omphalocele vs gastroschisis)

items.lo: ●●○ Discuss the appropriate use of prosthetic materials in hernia repair
items.text: Why is permanent synthetic mesh the default for elective groin-hernia repair but a riskier choice in a contaminated field?

items.lo: ●●○ Discuss the appropriate use of prosthetic materials in hernia repair
items.text: Standard repair = tension-free mesh (polypropylene/Marlex); techniques: Lichtenstein, plug-and-patch, Kugel, PHS — mesh lowers recurrence (mesh repair standard)

los.statement: ●●○ Describe the ano-rectal anatomy
●●● Describe the types, clinical presentation, and management of hemorrhoids
●●● Differentiate internal and external hemorrhoids
●●● Outline the clinical presentation and treatment of common perianal disorders, including perianal infections, fissures and fistula-in-ano
●●○ Describe the treatment of the different types of ano-rectal malignancies
●○○ Briefly discuss imperforate anus and rectal prolapse

items.lo: ●●○ Describe the ano-rectal anatomy
items.text: Why does the dentate line determine whether an anal lesion is painful and whether you can treat it without anesthesia?

items.lo: ●●○ Describe the ano-rectal anatomy
items.text: Dentate line = squamocolumnar junction AND somatic/visceral boundary; above = insensate mucosa, below = sensate anoderm; anal crypts = abscess origin

items.lo: ●●○ Describe the ano-rectal anatomy
items.text: Internal plexus (above) → portal drainage; external plexus (below) → caval drainage (plexus drainage)

items.lo: ●●● Describe the types, clinical presentation, and management of hemorrhoids
items.text: A symptomatic internal hemorrhoid has failed fiber and sitz baths — what office procedure fits, and why can it be done without anesthesia?

items.topic: GRADING — it is all about reducibility
items.lo: ●●● Describe the types, clinical presentation, and management of hemorrhoids
items.text: Grading: 1st no prolapse · 2nd spontaneous reduction · 3rd manual reduction · 4th irreducible (grade = reducibility)

items.lo: ●●● Describe the types, clinical presentation, and management of hemorrhoids
items.text: Ladder: conservative (fiber/softeners/sitz) → rubber-band ligation (internal, refractory, painless) → excisional hemorrhoidectomy (large III/IV)

items.lo: ●●● Differentiate internal and external hemorrhoids
items.text: A patient has painless bright-red rectal bleeding with a prolapsing mass; a second patient has acute severe anal pain with a tender lump — which hemorrhoid does each have, and what anatomic fact explains the difference?

items.lo: ●●● Differentiate internal and external hemorrhoids
items.text: Internal = above dentate, insensate mucosa, painless bleeding/prolapse; external = below dentate, sensate anoderm, acute pain when thrombosed (the dentate-line distinction)

items.lo: ●●● Outline the clinical presentation and treatment of common perianal disorders
items.text: A patient has a painful linear tear in the posterior midline with bright-red blood that is not mixed with the stool — what is the diagnosis, what finding would push you toward Crohn’s, and how do you treat it acutely?

items.lo: ●●● Outline the clinical presentation and treatment of common perianal disorders
items.text: Abscesses start in the anal crypts; perianal (infralevator) = most common; an untreated abscess → fistula-in-ano (crypt-to-skin tract, evaluate with anoscope/probe) (abscess → fistula)

items.lo: ●●● Outline the clinical presentation and treatment of common perianal disorders
items.text: Fissure-in-ano = painful posterior-midline linear ulcer, blood not mixed with stool, sentinel pile; multiple/atypical → Crohn’s; acute (<2 wk) → conservative (fissure pattern + Crohn’s flag)

items.lo: ●●● Outline the clinical presentation and treatment of common perianal disorders
items.text: STIs: condylomata (HPV, premalignant) · LGV (microimmunofluorescent titer, doxy) · gonorrhea (mucopurulent, culture+Gram, penicillin G+probenecid) · HSV (acyclovir) · syphilis (chancre) (anorectal STI differential)

items.lo: ●●○ Describe the treatment of the different types of ano-rectal malignancies
items.text: What is the modern treatment of choice for anal squamous cell carcinoma, and how did it change practice?

items.lo: ●●○ Describe the treatment of the different types of ano-rectal malignancies
items.text: Anal SCC: <2% of anorectal CA, equal frequency above/below dentate, poor prognosis; modern tx = chemoradiation (Nigro), sphincter-sparing (APR reserved for failures) (the APR → chemoradiation shift)

items.lo: ●●○ Describe the treatment of the different types of ano-rectal malignancies
items.text: Anorectal melanoma = rare, aggressive, may show black pigment; don’t mistake it for a thrombosed hemorrhoid (melanoma anchor)

items.lo: ●○○ Briefly discuss imperforate anus and rectal prolapse
items.text: Imperforate anus = neonatal malformation (meconium via fistula), surgical; rectal prolapse = pelvic-floor weakness — a chronic prolapse can harbor carcinoma (brief recall + prolapse-carcinoma vigilance)
